name: GATE 2027 CSE — Complete Preparation Guide
description: A rigorous 44-week, 1,341-hour dual-track preparation plan for GATE CSE 2027. Track A (21:30–23:30 weekdays) covers 156 lectures across Engineering Mathematics and Theory subjects. Track B (20:30–21:30 weekdays + full weekends) covers ~296 lectures across Core CS subjects with 79 DPPs. Includes 17 Track A DPPs, 5 subject assessments, and 10 full 3-hour mock exams. Target: AIR < 200, 80+ raw marks.
prerequisites: B.Tech CS/IT or equivalent undergraduate background
Ability to commit 31 hrs/week — 3 hrs/weekday + 8 hrs Saturday + 8 hrs Sunday
Access to GATE Overflow, Made Easy or ACE Academy mock test series

sections.name: All Subjects Combined (50 Qs — all Track A + B)
Week 1: DBMS + OS + C + DS + Algorithms (target ≥86%)
Week 2: CN + COA + Digital Logic + GA (tiered targets)
Week 3: Eng Math + DM + TOC + Compiler (target ≥80%)
General Aptitude — Attempt FIRST (10 Qs, 15 marks, 20 min)
Core CS — Tier 1: DBMS→OS→DSA→CN, Tier 2: Math→COA→Algo, Tier 3: Compiler→DM→TOC→DL

stages.name: Phase 3C — Final Sprint (Half-Mock, last study activity)

sections.name: Half-Mock: 33 Questions under full exam conditions

topics.name: DBMS 1: ER Model & Normalization
topics.description: ER model (entities, strong/weak, cardinality 1:1/1:N/M:N, participation constraints), ER-to-schema translation, and Normalization (1NF–BCNF). Covers ~10 lectures + DPP 1 (10 normalization PYQs on paper). Core GATE topic: BCNF decomposition and lossless join test.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: Normalization PYQs (10 questions on paper)
GATE PYQ: BCNF Decomposition

learningTip: **ER Translation Rule:** Weak entity set always gets a table with the owning entity's PK as part of its own PK.
**BCNF Check:** For every non-trivial FD X→Y, verify X is a superkey. If not, decompose — but BCNF does NOT always preserve FDs, unlike 3NF.
**Lossless Join Test:** R1∩R2 → R1 or R1∩R2 → R2. If either holds, the decomposition is lossless. This is a 1-2 mark GATE question annually.
mustNotMiss: **1NF:** Atomic values, no repeating groups. **2NF:** No partial dependency (non-key → part of composite PK). **3NF:** No transitive dependency. **BCNF:** Every determinant is a superkey.
**Lossless Join Condition:** R1∩R2 must be a superkey of R1 or R2.
**Dependency Preservation:** 3NF always preserves all FDs; BCNF may not — trade-off to know cold.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between 3NF and BCNF?
interviewQuestions.answer: **3NF:** For every FD X→A, either X is a superkey OR A is prime (part of some candidate key). Guarantees FD preservation + lossless join. \n**BCNF:** Every determinant must be a superkey. Stronger — eliminates all redundancy — but may NOT preserve all FDs. Prefer BCNF for clean design; use 3NF when FD preservation is required.

interviewQuestions.question: A relation R(A,B,C,D) has FDs: AB→C, C→D, D→A. Find all candidate keys.
interviewQuestions.answer: Check what determines all attributes. AB→C→D→A. So AB+ = {A,B,C,D}. CB→? C→D→A, so CB+ = {A,B,C,D}. DB→? D→A, so DB+ = {A,B,C,D}. Candidate Keys: **{AB, BC, BD}**.

topics.name: DBMS 2: Relational Algebra & SQL
topics.description: Relational algebra operators (σ, π, ×, ⋈, ⋈θ, ÷) and SQL — all join types, GROUP BY/HAVING, aggregates, correlated vs non-correlated subqueries, EXISTS/NOT EXISTS. Covers ~10 lectures + DPP 2 (10 transaction + SQL PYQs on paper).

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 2: Transaction + SQL PYQs (10 questions on paper)
GATE PYQ: Relational Algebra Expression

learningTip: **Division operator (÷):** R÷S gives tuples in R that are associated with ALL tuples in S. Think: 'students who enrolled in ALL courses'. Rare but appears in GATE.
**Correlated vs Non-correlated subquery:** Correlated re-executes for each outer row (slow). Non-correlated executes once. EXISTS is typically correlated.
**SQL execution order (not write order):** FROM → ON → JOIN → WHERE → GROUP BY → HAVING → SELECT → DISTINCT → ORDER BY → LIMIT.
mustNotMiss: **Natural Join vs Equijoin:** Natural Join auto-joins on same-named columns and removes duplicates; Equijoin keeps both join columns.
**NULL in aggregates:** COUNT(*) includes NULLs; COUNT(col) and SUM/AVG ignore NULLs.
**HAVING vs WHERE:** WHERE filters before grouping; HAVING filters after. Cannot use aggregate functions in WHERE.

interviewQuestions.question: Translate: 'Find names of students who have taken ALL courses offered by CS dept'.
interviewQuestions.answer: Use Division operator: π(sid, cid)(Enrollment) ÷ π(cid)(Courses WHERE dept='CS'). In SQL: use double NOT EXISTS — 'no CS course exists that the student did NOT enroll in'.

interviewQuestions.question: What is conflict serializability and how do you check it?
interviewQuestions.answer: A schedule is conflict-serializable if its **precedence graph is acyclic**. For each conflicting pair of operations (same data, at least one write, different transactions), add edge Ti→Tj. If graph has a cycle → NOT conflict-serializable. Cycle-free → find any topological order → that is the equivalent serial schedule.

topics.name: DBMS 3: Transactions, 2PL & Indexing + Final PYQ
topics.description: ACID properties, serializability (conflict + view), 2PL (growing/shrinking/strict/conservative), B+ trees (all records in leaves, leaves linked), dense vs sparse indexes, multilevel indexing. Covers ~10 lectures + DPP 3 + Final GATE PYQ test (55 min, target ≥90%).

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 3: 2PL + Indexing PYQs (final DPP)
Final PYQ Test: All GATE DBMS 2015–2026 (55 min, target ≥90%)

learningTip: **2PL Rule:** In growing phase, only acquire locks. In shrinking phase, only release. Once you release any lock, you CANNOT acquire new locks. Strict 2PL: release all write locks only at commit/abort.
**B+ Tree vs B-Tree:** B+ stores ALL data in leaf nodes (leaf linked list for range queries). B-tree stores data in internal nodes too — but GATE almost exclusively tests B+ trees.
**Gate target:** DBMS PYQ ≥90%. DBMS is high-yield — typically 5–7 marks in GATE CS.
mustNotMiss: **ACID:** Atomicity (all/none), Consistency (constraints preserved), Isolation (no dirty reads), Durability (survives crash).
**Dense index:** One entry per search key value. **Sparse index:** One entry per block (only for primary/clustered index on ordered file).
**B+ Tree order m:** Max m-1 keys, min ⌈m/2⌉-1 keys per internal node (except root). Leaves: min ⌈(m-1)/2⌉ keys.

interviewQuestions.question: Why does Strict 2PL prevent cascading rollbacks but basic 2PL does not?
interviewQuestions.answer: Basic 2PL can release write locks before commit, allowing another transaction to read dirty (uncommitted) data. If T1 rolls back, T2 must also rollback (cascading). **Strict 2PL:** Write locks held until commit/abort — no transaction can read uncommitted data, so cascading rollback is impossible.

interviewQuestions.question: A B+ tree of order 5 has 100 leaf nodes. What is the maximum number of records it can index?
interviewQuestions.answer: Order 5 → max (5-1)=4 keys per leaf. 100 leaf nodes × 4 = **400 records** maximum.

topics.name: C 1: Pointers, Storage Classes & Undefined Behaviour
topics.description: ~12 lectures + DPPs 1–7 (output prediction, trace on paper). Pointer declarations (int *p, int **p, int (*fp)(int), function pointers), pointer arithmetic (p+1 = p+sizeof(*p)), void* casting, storage classes (auto/static/extern/register), struct padding, union, sizeof traps, macro pitfalls, and undefined behaviour.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 1–7: Output Prediction (trace every variable on paper)
GATE PYQ: C Pointers and Storage

learningTip: **DPP Rule (non-negotiable):** Trace EVERY program — write every variable's value at every step on paper. Never eyeball output. One missed step = wrong answer.
**Macro trap:** `#define SQ(x) x*x` → SQ(2+3) = 2+3*2+3 = 11, NOT 25. Always parenthesize: `#define SQ(x) ((x)*(x))`.
**sizeof trap:** `sizeof(array)` gives full array size. `sizeof(pointer)` gives 4/8 bytes. Passing array to function decays to pointer — size information lost.
mustNotMiss: **int *a[5] vs int (*a)[5]:** First is array of 5 int-pointers. Second is pointer to an array of 5 ints.
**Static local variable:** Initialized once, retains value between function calls. Stored in BSS/data segment, not stack.
**Undefined Behaviour (UB):** Read before init, signed integer overflow, double-free, dereferencing NULL, accessing freed memory — all UB. Compiler can do anything.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the output? `int a[] = {1,2,3,4,5}; int *p = a+2; printf("%d", *(p-1) + *(p+1));`
Explain struct padding with an example.
interviewQuestions.answer: Each member aligns to its own size. `struct {char c; int i;}` → c at offset 0, 3 bytes padding, i at offset 4. Total: 8 bytes (not 5). Reorder to `struct {int i; char c;}` for 8 bytes, but `char` then has 3 bytes tail padding. Minimize waste by ordering members largest-to-smallest.

topics.name: C 2: Output Prediction, Bitwise & All DPPs + Final PYQ
topics.description: ~12 lectures + DPPs 8–13 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥88%). Topics: bitwise operations (x&(x-1) clears lowest set bit, x|(x-1) sets bits below LSB), recursion traces, function call order, precedence traps, all GATE C PYQs 2015–2026.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 8–13: Advanced output prediction on paper
Final PYQ: All GATE C PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥88%)

learningTip: **Bitwise identity:** x & (x-1) clears the lowest set bit of x. Use case: count set bits (Brian Kernighan). x | (x-1) sets all bits from LSB downward.
**Post/pre-increment in expressions:** `i++ + ++i` — behaviour depends on sequence points. In GATE, such questions test whether you know it's UB or not.
**Gate target: PYQ ≥88%. All 13 DPPs fully traced on paper.** C typically contributes 4–6 marks in GATE.
mustNotMiss: **x & (x-1):** Clears lowest set bit. Count set bits: while(n) { count++; n &= (n-1); }
**Operator precedence:** `*p++` means *(p++) — dereference THEN increment pointer. `(*p)++` increments the value.
**Recursion trace:** Always draw the call stack. Return value flows BACK — do not assume a recursive function 'returns' immediately.

interviewQuestions.question: How many set bits does 52 have? Use Brian Kernighan's method.
What does `static` do inside a function vs outside?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Inside function:** Variable persists across calls (stored in data segment, not stack). Initialized once. \n**Outside function (file scope):** Limits linkage to the current file (internal linkage) — invisible to other .c files.

topics.name: DS 1: Arrays, Linked Lists & BST
topics.description: ~14 lectures + DPPs 1–2. 2D array row-major addressing (base+(i×C+j)×esize), LL reversal (3-pointer + recursive), Floyd's cycle detection (tortoise-hare), BST operations — especially 3-case deletion (draw 5 two-child deletions explicitly).

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: Arrays + Linked Lists on paper
DPP 2: BST operations and traversals

learningTip: **Row-major address formula:** Address of A[i][j] = Base + (i×NumCols + j) × element_size. For column-major: Base + (j×NumRows + i) × element_size.
**BST deletion (2-child case):** Replace with in-order successor (leftmost of right subtree) OR in-order predecessor. Then delete the successor/predecessor from its original position.
**Floyd's cycle detection:** Slow pointer moves 1 step, fast moves 2. If they meet → cycle exists. Start of cycle: reset one pointer to head, move both 1 step at a time until they meet.
mustNotMiss: **2D Array row-major:** A[i][j] at offset i×C+j. Column-major: j×R+i. GATE asks both!
**BST in-order traversal** gives sorted sequence. Pre-order = root-left-right (used to reconstruct tree). Post-order: left-right-root.
**Floyd Cycle Detection:** Time O(n), Space O(1). Finding cycle start requires second phase.

interviewQuestions.question: A 5×4 integer array starts at address 1000. Find address of A[3][2] in row-major order. (int = 4 bytes)
How do you detect and find the start of a cycle in a linked list in O(n) time, O(1) space?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Phase 1:** Slow=Fast=head. Move slow by 1, fast by 2. If they meet → cycle. \n**Phase 2:** Reset slow to head. Move both by 1. They meet at **cycle start**. Proof: distance from head to cycle start = distance from meeting point to cycle start (mod cycle length).

topics.name: DS 2: Trees & AVL Rotations
topics.description: ~14 lectures + DPPs 3–4. AVL trees (BF = h(left)−h(right) ∈ {−1,0,1}, all 4 rotations from memory: LL/RR/LR/RL), B-trees (order m: max m−1 keys, min ⌈m/2⌉−1 keys), infix→postfix conversion with 3 nested examples. Gate target: AVL rotation < 3 min.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 3: AVL insertion and rotation on paper
DPP 4: B-tree and expression trees

learningTip: **AVL Rotation Trigger:** After insertion, walk UP from inserted node. First unbalanced ancestor is the pivot. Identify rotation type from the path: Left-Left ↠ Right rotation. Right-Right ↠ Left rotation. Left-Right ↠ Left then Right. Right-Left ↠ Right then Left.
**LR Rotation mnemonic:** The middle element of the 3-node chain becomes the new root. Always draw the 3 nodes and 4 subtrees — then redistribute.
**Gate target: AVL rotation < 3 min cold.** Practice until you can do 5 insertions into an AVL tree without looking at notes.
mustNotMiss: **BF = Height(left subtree) − Height(right subtree).** Height of null = -1. BF must stay in {-1, 0, +1} at every node.
**B-tree of order m:** Internal node max m children, max m-1 keys, min ⌈m/2⌉ children (except root min 2). All leaves at same level.
**Infix → Postfix:** Use a stack. Operand → output immediately. Operator → pop operators of higher/equal precedence, then push. ')' pops till '('.

interviewQuestions.question: Insert 10, 20, 30 into an initially empty AVL tree. Show the rotation.
interviewQuestions.answer: Insert 10 (root). Insert 20 (right of 10). Insert 30: 10.BF=-2 (Right-Right case). Apply **Left rotation** at 10. 20 becomes root, 10 is left child, 30 is right child. BF of all nodes = 0. ✓

interviewQuestions.question: What is the minimum number of nodes in an AVL tree of height h?
interviewQuestions.answer: Let N(h) = min nodes in AVL tree of height h. N(0)=1, N(1)=2. Recurrence: **N(h) = N(h-1) + N(h-2) + 1** (one root, one subtree of height h-1, one of h-2). This gives Fibonacci-like growth.

topics.name: DS 3: Heaps, Hashing & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~14 lectures + DPPs 5–6 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥88%). Max-heap (build O(n) via heapify-down from n/2 to 1), heap sort trace, hashing — chaining (load factor α=n/m), open addressing (linear/quadratic/double hashing), collision analysis.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 5–6: Heap + Hashing PYQs on paper
Final PYQ: All GATE DS PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥88%, AVL < 3 min)

learningTip: **Build Heap O(n):** Start heapify-down from index n/2 (last non-leaf) down to index 1 (root). Each heapify-down is O(log n) but total is O(n) — most heapify-downs on small subtrees.
**Hash chaining:** Expected search time = O(1 + α) where α = n/m (load factor). Degrades to O(n) in worst case when all keys hash to same slot.

**Open Addressing:** Linear probing: probe i+1, i+2... Suffers from **primary clustering**. Quadratic probing: i+1², i+2²... **Secondary clustering**. Double hashing: h(k,i) = h1(k) + i×h2(k). Best distribution.
mustNotMiss: **Heap property:** In max-heap, parent ≥ children. Array representation: parent of i = ⌊i/2⌋. Left child = 2i. Right child = 2i+1.
**Heap sort:** O(n log n) time, O(1) space (in-place). Not stable.
**Load factor α:** Chaining works fine at α > 1. Open addressing requires α < 1 (table can fill up).

interviewQuestions.question: Why is building a heap O(n) and not O(n log n)?
interviewQuestions.answer: When heapify-down runs on nodes at height h from leaf level, each call costs O(h). Most nodes are near the leaves (small h). Sum = Σ (n/2^(h+1)) × h for h=0..log n. This telescoping sum converges to **O(n)**, not O(n log n).

interviewQuestions.question: In linear probing with load factor 0.5, what is the expected number of probes for unsuccessful search?
interviewQuestions.answer: For linear probing: Expected probes (unsuccessful) = ½(1 + 1/(1-α)²). At α=0.5: ½(1 + 1/0.25) = ½×5 = **2.5 probes**.

topics.name: Algorithms 1: Complexity, Sorting & Graph Traversal
topics.description: ~16 lectures + DPPs 1–3. Big-O/Theta/Omega definitions, Master Theorem (3 cases, 10 recurrences cold), all comparison-based sorts (merge O(n log n), quicksort worst O(n²), heapsort O(n log n)), lower bound Ω(n log n) for comparison-based sorting, BFS/DFS (O(V+E)), edge types, topological sort.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: Recurrences using Master Theorem
DPPs 2–3: Sorting and graph traversal PYQs

learningTip: **Master Theorem 3 cases:** T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n). Compare f(n) with n^(log_b a). Case 1: f smaller → T = Θ(n^log_b_a). Case 2: f equal → T = Θ(n^log_b_a · log n). Case 3: f larger (+ regularity) → T = Θ(f(n)). Gate target: solve any case < 20 sec.
**DFS timestamps:** d[v] (discovery) and f[v] (finish). Back edge: d[u] > d[v] and f[u] < f[v] (ancestor). Cross edge: d[u] > d[v] and f[u] > f[v].
**Topological Sort:** Valid only for DAG. DFS-based: push to stack on finish. Kahn's (BFS-based): start from 0 in-degree nodes.
mustNotMiss: **Sorting complexity table:** Merge: O(n log n)/O(n). Quick: avg O(n log n)/O(log n), worst O(n²)/O(n). Heap: O(n log n)/O(1). Insertion: O(n²)/O(1) — stable.
**Lower bound:** Any comparison-based sort requires Ω(n log n) comparisons in worst case. Radix/counting sort beats this by not using comparisons.
**DFS tree edge classification:** Tree (new), Back (ancestor), Forward (descendant), Cross (neither). In undirected graph, only tree and back edges exist.

interviewQuestions.question: Solve T(n) = 3T(n/2) + n².
interviewQuestions.answer: a=3, b=2, f(n)=n². n^(log_b a) = n^(log_2 3) ≈ n^1.58. f(n) = n² grows faster. Check regularity: 3(n/2)² = 3n²/4 ≤ cn² for c=3/4 < 1 ✓. **Case 3: T(n) = Θ(n²).**

interviewQuestions.question: Quick sort performs poorly on already-sorted input with pivot = first element. Why and how to fix?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pivot = first element on sorted array → largest partition split (n-1 vs 0). Recurrence becomes T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) → **O(n²)**. Fix: **Random pivot** (expected O(n log n)) or **Median-of-3 pivot** (first, middle, last).

topics.name: Algorithms 2: Graph Algorithms & Shortest Paths
topics.description: ~16 lectures + DPP 4. Dijkstra O((V+E) log V) — no negative edges. Bellman-Ford O(VE) — detects negative cycles. Floyd-Warshall O(V³) — all-pairs. MST: Kruskal (sort edges, union-find) + Prim (greedy, min-heap). Cut property for MST correctness.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 4: SSSP and MST problems on paper
GATE PYQ: MST and Dijkstra questions

learningTip: **Dijkstra's greedy proof:** At each step, the vertex u extracted from the min-heap has the correct shortest path (assuming non-negative weights). Negative edges break this — use Bellman-Ford.
**Bellman-Ford negative cycle:** Run V-1 relaxations. If ANY edge can still be relaxed on the Vth iteration → negative cycle detected.
**MST Cut Property:** For any cut (S, V-S), the minimum weight edge crossing the cut is in some MST. This is why Prim's and Kruskal's are correct.
mustNotMiss: **Dijkstra time complexity:** Simple array = O(V²). Binary heap = O((V+E) log V). Fibonacci heap = O(E + V log V). GATE uses O((V+E) log V).
**Bellman-Ford:** Does V-1 iterations over all E edges. Total: O(VE). Can handle negative weights, detects negative cycles.
**Floyd-Warshall recurrence:** D[i][j][k] = min(D[i][j][k-1], D[i][k][k-1] + D[k][j][k-1]). O(V³) time, O(V²) space.

interviewQuestions.question: When does Kruskal's algorithm fail to produce a correct MST?
interviewQuestions.answer: Kruskal's **never fails** on connected graphs — it's provably correct by the Cut Property. However, it requires a connected graph. For disconnected graphs, it produces a Minimum Spanning **Forest** (one MST per component).

interviewQuestions.question: Can Dijkstra handle graphs with negative weight edges if there are no negative cycles?
interviewQuestions.answer: **No.** Even with no negative cycles, Dijkstra can produce wrong answers with negative edges. Once a vertex is 'settled' (extracted from heap), Dijkstra assumes its distance is final — a later negative edge could provide a shorter path. Use **Bellman-Ford** for negative edges.

topics.name: Algorithms 3: Greedy, DP & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~16 lectures + DPPs 5–8 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥88%, Master Theorem < 20 sec). Greedy: activity selection, fractional knapsack, Huffman. DP: 0/1 Knapsack (draw n×W table), LCS (draw 2D table), LIS O(n²), Matrix Chain Multiplication.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 5–6: Greedy + DP table construction on paper
Final PYQ: All GATE Algorithms PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥88%)

learningTip: **Greedy vs DP choice:** If locally optimal choice leads to globally optimal → Greedy. If optimal substructure + overlapping subproblems → DP. 0/1 Knapsack CANNOT be solved greedily (fractional can).
**LCS table recall:** dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]+1 if X[i]==Y[j], else max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]). Always draw the table in GATE — don't compute in head.
**Huffman:** Build min-heap of frequencies. Always merge the two smallest. Result: most frequent character gets shortest code. Greedy choice is optimal (proven by exchange argument).
mustNotMiss: **0/1 Knapsack DP:** O(nW) time and space where n=items, W=capacity. NOT polynomial (W can be exponentially large in input bits).
**Activity Selection:** Sort by finish time. Greedily pick earliest-finishing compatible activity. Optimal by exchange argument.
**Matrix Chain Multiplication:** dp[i][j] = min cost to multiply matrices i..j. O(n³) time. Order of multiplication matters; parenthesization does not change the result values.

interviewQuestions.question: Find LCS of 'ABCBDAB' and 'BDCAB'.
interviewQuestions.answer: Build the 7×5 DP table. LCS length = **4**. One LCS = 'BCAB' or 'BDAB'. (Draw the complete table in exam — don't try to compute mentally.)

interviewQuestions.question: Is the Greedy algorithm optimal for 0/1 Knapsack? Give a counterexample.
interviewQuestions.answer: **No.** Greedy by value/weight ratio fails. Example: W=10, items: (6kg, ₹12), (5kg, ₹10), (5kg, ₹10). Ratio: 2.0, 2.0, 2.0. Greedy picks (6kg,₹12) then only 4kg left (neither fits) → ₹12. Optimal: pick two (5kg,₹10) items → ₹20. DP is required.

topics.name: OS 1: Process States & CPU Scheduling
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPP 1. Process state machine (5 states + transitions), all scheduling algorithms: FCFS, SJF, SRTF (preemptive SJF), Round Robin (Q=3), Priority. Draw Gantt charts for P1(0,10) P2(1,1) P3(2,2) P4(3,1) P5(4,5). Compute avg waiting time and turnaround time for each.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: CPU Scheduling Gantt charts on paper
GATE PYQ: Scheduling wait time calculations

learningTip: **Draw Gantt charts on paper — always.** GATE scheduling questions require exact wait/turnaround times. One wrong Gantt = wrong answer. Never compute in head.
**SRTF (preemptive SJF):** When a new process arrives, compare its burst time with the REMAINING burst of the current process. Preempt if new < remaining.
**Turnaround = Completion − Arrival. Waiting = Turnaround − Burst.** These two formulas solve all scheduling problems.
mustNotMiss: **5 process states:** New → Ready → Running → Waiting → Terminated. Running→Ready (preemption/time quantum). Running→Waiting (I/O). Waiting→Ready (I/O complete).
**RR scheduling:** Maintains a FIFO ready queue. On quantum expiry, process moves to END of queue. New arrivals join at end.
**Convoy effect:** In FCFS, one long process blocks all shorter processes (poor avg wait). SJF eliminates convoy effect.

interviewQuestions.question: For processes P1(at=0, bt=10), P2(at=1, bt=1), P3(at=2, bt=2) with SRTF, find avg waiting time.
interviewQuestions.answer: t=0: P1 runs. t=1: P2 arrives (bt=1 < P1 remaining=9) → P2 runs. t=2: P2 done. P3 arrives (bt=2 < P1 remaining=9) → P3 runs. t=4: P3 done. P1 resumes, finishes at t=13. WT: P1=13-10-0=3, P2=0, P3=2. Avg WT = **5/3 ≈ 1.67**.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between SJF and SRTF?
interviewQuestions.answer: **SJF (non-preemptive):** Once a process starts, it runs to completion. Picks shortest burst at each dispatch point. \n**SRTF (preemptive SJF):** On every new arrival AND on every preemption, compare new process burst with remaining burst of running process. Optimal for minimizing average waiting time.

topics.name: OS 2: Deadlocks & Banker's Algorithm
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPPs 2–3. Four necessary conditions for deadlock, Resource Allocation Graph (RAG), Banker's Safety Algorithm (full 5-process example), deadlock prevention/avoidance/detection/recovery. Gate target: Banker's algorithm complete < 5 min.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 2–3: Deadlock and Banker's algorithm problems
GATE PYQ: RAG cycle detection and safe state

learningTip: **Banker's Algorithm steps:** Available = Resources − Allocated. Need = Max − Allocated. Find a process where Need ≤ Available. 'Execute' it: Available += Allocated. Repeat. If all finish → safe state.
**RAG cycle rule:** Single instance resources: cycle → deadlock. Multi-instance: cycle → POSSIBLE deadlock (need further analysis). NO cycle → NO deadlock.
**Gate target: Banker's < 5 min.** Practice the matrix operations until mechanical.
mustNotMiss: **4 Necessary Conditions (from A3):** Mutual Exclusion · Hold and Wait · No Preemption · Circular Wait. ALL four must hold for deadlock. Break ANY one → prevent deadlock.
**Prevention vs Avoidance:** Prevention eliminates one of the 4 conditions (may reduce concurrency). Avoidance uses Banker's to ensure safe state (requires advance knowledge of max needs).
**Recovery options:** Process termination (kill all or one-by-one), Resource preemption (rollback, starvation risk).

interviewQuestions.question: Three processes P1(holds R1, needs R2), P2(holds R2, needs R3), P3(holds R3, needs R1). Deadlock?
interviewQuestions.answer: Draw RAG: P1→R2→P2→R3→P3→R1→P1. **Cycle exists with single-instance resources → Deadlock.** All four conditions are met: ME, Hold&Wait, No Preemption (assumed), Circular Wait (the cycle).

interviewQuestions.question: What is the key difference between deadlock prevention and deadlock avoidance?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Prevention:** Breaks one of the 4 necessary conditions statically (e.g., request all resources at once = no Hold&Wait). Simple but resource-wasteful. \n**Avoidance:** Checks each allocation request dynamically using Banker's algorithm to ensure system stays in a safe state. Requires priori knowledge of maximum needs.

topics.name: OS 3: Paging & Page Replacement
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPPs 4–5. Paging EAT (Effective Access Time), page replacement algorithms: FIFO (Belady's anomaly), LRU (optimal in practice), OPT (offline lower bound). Classic reference string: 1,2,3,4,1,2,5,1,2,3,4,5 with 3/4 frames.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 4–5: Paging and page replacement on paper
GATE PYQ: EAT and page fault calculations

learningTip: **EAT formula (no TLB):** EAT = p × (2m) + (1-p) × m, where p = page fault rate, m = memory access time. With TLB: EAT = h×m + (1-h)×(2m), where h = TLB hit rate.
**Belady's Anomaly:** FIFO can have MORE page faults with MORE frames. LRU and OPT do NOT suffer from Belady's anomaly (they are stack algorithms).
**OPT algorithm:** Replace the page that will be used farthest in the future. Not implementable in practice but gives lower bound for comparison.
mustNotMiss: **FIFO:** Suffers from Belady's anomaly (more frames → more faults in some cases). Simple but not optimal.
**LRU:** Replace the least recently used page. Stack algorithm (stack property): set of pages in memory with n frames ⊆ set with n+1 frames — NO Belady's anomaly.
**Page fault handling time:** Includes OS interrupt + page-in from disk + restart instruction. Disk access ≈ 8ms. Even 0.1% fault rate dominates EAT.

interviewQuestions.question: Simulate FIFO page replacement for reference string 1,2,3,4,1,2,5 with 3 frames.
interviewQuestions.answer: Frames: [1], [1,2], [1,2,3], [2,3,4](fault, evict 1), [2,3,4]→[3,4,1](fault, evict 2), [3,4,1]→[4,1,2](fault, evict 3), [4,1,2]→[1,2,5](fault, evict 4). Page faults = **5** (excluding initial compulsory faults: actual = 5 after first 3 loads).

interviewQuestions.question: TLB hit time = 10ns, memory access time = 100ns, TLB hit rate = 90%. Find EAT.

topics.name: OS 4: Concurrency, Semaphores & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPPs 6–8 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥90%, Banker's < 5 min). Binary and counting semaphores, P(wait) and V(signal) operations, classical problems: Producer-Consumer, Readers-Writers, Dining Philosophers. Critical section conditions (Mutual Exclusion, Progress, Bounded Waiting).

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 6–8: Semaphore programs and mutual exclusion
Final PYQ: All GATE OS PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥90%)

learningTip: **Semaphore P and V:** P(s): s--; if s<0 block. V(s): s++; if s≤0 wakeup one. Binary semaphore (mutex): initialized to 1. Counting semaphore: initialized to N (available resources).

**Producer-Consumer solution:** Uses 3 semaphores: mutex (init 1), empty (init N), full (init 0). Producer: P(empty), P(mutex), produce, V(mutex), V(full). Consumer: P(full), P(mutex), consume, V(mutex), V(empty).
**Gate target: OS PYQ ≥90%.** OS is typically 8–10 marks. Scheduling + Deadlock + Paging + Semaphores are the four tested clusters.
mustNotMiss: **Critical Section Requirements:** 1. Mutual Exclusion (only one in CS at a time). 2. Progress (if CS empty, decision made in finite time). 3. Bounded Waiting (no starvation).
**Counting semaphore:** Can represent a resource count. Negative value = number of waiting processes.
**Test-and-Set (hardware):** Atomic instruction. while(test_and_set(&lock)); — busy wait (spinlock). Not bounded-waiting by itself without queuing.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the difference between a binary semaphore and a mutex?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Binary Semaphore:** Can be signaled by ANY process, not just the one that locked it. Used for signaling between processes. \n**Mutex (mutual exclusion lock):** Has the concept of ownership — only the thread that locked it can unlock it. Used specifically for critical section protection.

interviewQuestions.question: Can deadlock occur with semaphores? Give an example.
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes. P1: P(S1), P(S2). P2: P(S2), P(S1). If P1 acquires S1 and P2 acquires S2 simultaneously, both block waiting for the other's semaphore. **Deadlock.** Fix: always acquire semaphores in the same order in all processes.

topics.name: CN 1: OSI Layers, Error & Flow Control
topics.description: ~13 lectures + DPPs 1–2. OSI 7-layer + TCP/IP 4-layer model, CRC computation by hand (2 examples), Hamming distance for error detection/correction, stop-and-wait, Go-Back-N, Selective Repeat window sizes and efficiencies.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 1–2: CRC + Flow control efficiency problems
GATE PYQ: GBN and SR window size

learningTip: **Memorise before first lecture (from guide):** η_SW = 1/(1+2a). GBN window ≤ 2^n − 1. SR window ≤ 2^(n-1). Where a = propagation delay / transmission time.
**Efficiency formula:** If W ≥ 1+2a, link is fully utilized → η=1. If W < 1+2a, η = W/(1+2a).
**CRC division:** Perform binary XOR division (no carries). Remainder = CRC checksum. Receiver divides (data+CRC) — if remainder = 0, no error detected.
mustNotMiss: **From A3 sheet:** η_SW=1/(1+2a). GBN ≤ 2^n−1. SR ≤ 2^(n-1).
**Hamming distance:** Min Hamming distance d → can detect d-1 errors, correct ⌊(d-1)/2⌋ errors.
**GBN vs SR:** GBN retransmits window-size packets on error. SR only retransmits the errored packet. SR needs larger receiver buffer.

interviewQuestions.question: n=3 bits for sequence numbers. What is max window size for GBN vs SR?
interviewQuestions.answer: Sequence numbers: 2^3 = 8 (0–7). **GBN max window = 2^3 − 1 = 7.** **SR max window = 2^(3-1) = 4.** SR restricts to half to avoid ambiguity between old and new frames on wrap-around.

interviewQuestions.question: Propagation delay = 20ms, transmission time = 1ms. What is stop-and-wait efficiency?
interviewQuestions.answer: a = propagation/transmission = 20/1 = 20. η = 1/(1+2×20) = 1/41 ≈ **2.4%**. Link is severely underutilized — use sliding window protocol.

topics.name: CN 2: IP, ALOHA, Subnetting & Routing
topics.description: ~13 lectures + DPPs 3–5 + 5 subnetting problems. ALOHA efficiency derivations, CSMA/CD min frame size, IP classful + CIDR + VLSM subnetting, routing protocols (RIP/Bellman-Ford, OSPF/Dijkstra, BGP/path-vector). Gate target: subnetting < 2 min.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 3–4: ALOHA + Subnetting (5 problems each)
DPP 5: Routing protocol problems

learningTip: **From A3 sheet:** Pure ALOHA max throughput ≈ 18.4% (at G=0.5, e^(-2G)=1/2e). Slotted ALOHA max ≈ 36.8% (at G=1, Ge^(-G)=1/e). Both use S = Ge^(-2G) or Ge^(-G).
**Subnetting shortcut:** /n subnet → 2^(32-n) total addresses, 2^(32-n)-2 usable hosts. For /27: 32 addresses, 30 hosts. For /30: 4 addresses, 2 hosts (point-to-point links).
**CSMA/CD min frame size:** Transmission time ≥ 2 × propagation delay. So min frame size = 2 × propagation delay × bandwidth. In Ethernet: 64 bytes (collision detected by then).
mustNotMiss: **Classful IP:** Class A: 0-127 (/8). Class B: 128-191 (/16). Class C: 192-223 (/24). Class D: Multicast. Class E: Reserved.
**RIP vs OSPF:** RIP uses Bellman-Ford (distance vector), max 15 hops, slow convergence. OSPF uses Dijkstra (link-state), no hop limit, fast convergence.
**BGP:** Path-vector protocol (TCP port 179). Used between autonomous systems (inter-AS routing). Policy-based.

interviewQuestions.question: Network 192.168.1.0/24. Subnet into 4 equal subnets. Give the subnet addresses.
Why does slotted ALOHA have twice the throughput of pure ALOHA?
interviewQuestions.answer: Pure ALOHA: vulnerable period = 2T (frame can collide if another starts within T before or after). S = Ge^(-2G). \nSlotted ALOHA: transmit only at slot boundaries → vulnerable period = T. S = Ge^(-G). At optimum G: pure max = 1/(2e) ≈ 18.4%, slotted max = 1/e ≈ 36.8%.

topics.name: CN 3: TCP, Application Layer & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~12 lectures + DPPs 6–7 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥88%, subnetting < 2 min). TCP 3-way handshake, congestion control (slow start, congestion avoidance, fast retransmit), UDP. Application layer: DNS, HTTP, FTP, SMTP.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 6–7: TCP + Application layer problems
Final PYQ: All GATE CN PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥88%)

learningTip: **TCP 3-way handshake:** SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK. Client→Server: SYN (seq=x). Server→Client: SYN-ACK (seq=y, ack=x+1). Client→Server: ACK (seq=x+1, ack=y+1). Connection established.

**TCP congestion control:** Slow start (cwnd doubles per RTT until ssthresh). Congestion avoidance (linear increase). On timeout: ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=1. On 3 dup ACKs (fast retransmit): ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=ssthresh.
**DNS hierarchy:** Root → TLD (.com, .in) → Authoritative. Recursive vs iterative queries. TTL for caching.
mustNotMiss: **TCP vs UDP:** TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented, flow/congestion control, 20-byte header. UDP: unreliable, unordered, connectionless, 8-byte header. Used for video streaming, DNS.
**Well-known ports:** HTTP:80, HTTPS:443, FTP:21(control)/20(data), SSH:22, DNS:53, SMTP:25, Telnet:23.
**HTTP methods:** GET (idempotent, cached), POST (not idempotent, not cached), PUT (update), DELETE.

interviewQuestions.question: TCP window size = 65535 bytes, RTT = 100ms. What is max throughput?
interviewQuestions.answer: Max throughput = Window size / RTT = 65535 bytes / 0.1s = 655,350 bytes/s ≈ **5.24 Mbps**. TCP throughput is limited by both window size and RTT.

interviewQuestions.question: Explain TCP's slow start phase. When does it end?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Slow start:** Initially cwnd=1 MSS. cwnd doubles every RTT (adds 1 MSS per ACK received). Ends when: (1) cwnd reaches ssthresh → switches to congestion avoidance, or (2) timeout/loss occurs → ssthresh=cwnd/2, cwnd=1, restart.

topics.name: COA 1: Number Systems, IEEE 754 & Performance
topics.description: ~10 lectures + DPPs 1–2. 2's complement (overflow: carry_in_MSB ≠ carry_out_MSB), IEEE 754 single/double precision, IC×CPI×T_clk performance equation, Amdahl's law: Speedup = 1/((1−f)+f/k). 5 Amdahl problems.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 1–2: 2's complement + Amdahl problems on paper
GATE PYQ: IEEE 754 and performance calculations

learningTip: **Write AMAT formula at top of every COA page:** AMAT = L1_hit + L1_miss_rate × (L2_hit + L2_miss_rate × MM_time).
**2's complement overflow:** Overflow occurs iff carry INTO MSB ≠ carry OUT OF MSB. Positive + Positive = Negative → overflow. Negative + Negative = Positive → overflow.
**Amdahl speed limit:** Even with k=∞ cores, speedup ≤ 1/(1−f). If 20% is serial, max speedup = 5x regardless of parallelism.
mustNotMiss: **IEEE 754 single:** 1 sign + 8 exponent (biased-127) + 23 mantissa. Value = (−1)^s × 1.mantissa × 2^(exp−127). Special: exp=255 → Inf/NaN.
**Performance equation:** CPU time = IC × CPI × Clock period. Decrease any of IC (compiler/algorithm), CPI (architecture), Clock period (hardware).
**From A3:** Amdahl: Speedup = 1/((1−f)+f/k). Pipeline speedup = n×k/(k+n−1) where n=stages, k=tasks.

interviewQuestions.question: 70% of a program can be parallelized. With 4 processors, what is the speedup?
Represent -7 in 4-bit 2's complement. Verify addition -7 + 3.

topics.name: COA 2: Pipelining & Hazards
topics.description: ~10 lectures + DPPs 3–5. 5-stage pipeline (IF/ID/EX/MEM/WB), CPI=1 ideal, pipeline time=(k+n−1)×T_clk, RAW/WAR/WAW data hazards, control hazards (branch penalty), forwarding (eliminates most RAW except load-use = 1 stall), draw timing diagrams for 6+ instructions.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 3–5: Pipeline diagrams and hazard analysis on paper
GATE PYQ: Pipeline CPI with stalls

learningTip: **From A3:** CPI = 1 + stalls_per_instruction. Throughput = 1 / max_stage_time. Speedup = n × k / (k + n − 1) for k-stage pipeline, n tasks.
**Load-use hazard:** LOAD R1, mem followed by ADD R2, R1, R3 → 1 stall even with forwarding. Forwarding only delivers result AFTER MEM stage but ADD needs it at EX start.
**WAR and WAW** are not true data hazards in simple pipelines (resolved by in-order execution). RAW is the critical hazard.
mustNotMiss: **3 hazard types:** RAW (read-after-write, true dependency), WAR (write-after-read, anti-dependency), WAW (write-after-write, output dependency).
**Forwarding paths:** EX/MEM → EX input, MEM/WB → EX input. Eliminates most RAW stalls. Load-use: EX result not available until end of MEM → 1 stall required.
**Branch prediction:** Assume not taken → pipeline continues. On branch taken → flush (branch penalty). Static prediction vs dynamic (1-bit, 2-bit saturating counter).

interviewQuestions.question: 5-stage pipeline, 100 instructions, k=5 stages, each stage 2ns. Time with and without pipeline?
interviewQuestions.answer: Without pipeline: 100 × 5 × 2 = 1000 ns. With pipeline: (5+100-1) × 2 = 104 × 2 = **208 ns**. Speedup = 1000/208 ≈ 4.8× (not 5× due to fill/drain overhead).

interviewQuestions.question: Explain load-use hazard with forwarding. Why is 1 stall still needed?
interviewQuestions.answer: LOAD writes result to register at END of MEM stage. The next instruction (ADD) needs that register at the START of its EX stage. Even with forwarding (MEM/WB → EX), the data arrives 1 cycle late — ADD's EX begins before LOAD's MEM ends. **1 pipeline stall (bubble) is unavoidable.**

topics.name: COA 3: Cache Memory & Virtual Memory
topics.description: ~10 lectures + DPPs 6–9. Cache: direct-mapped/fully-associative/k-way set-associative, AMAT formula, write-through vs write-back. Virtual Memory: VPN→PFN translation, 2-level page tables (3 memory accesses), TLB EAT. 8 AMAT problems + 5 EAT problems. Write AMAT formula before every problem.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 6–8: AMAT calculations (8 problems on paper)
DPP 9: TLB EAT calculations (5 problems)

learningTip: **From A3 (write before every problem):** AMAT = L1_hit_time + L1_miss_rate × (L2_hit_time + L2_miss_rate × MM_access_time).
**TLB EAT (from A3):** Without TLB: 2m (page table access + data access). With TLB: EAT = h×(t_TLB + m) + (1-h)×(t_TLB + m + m). Simplify: h×(t_TLB+m) + (1-h)×(t_TLB+2m).
**2-level page table:** 3 memory accesses per data reference (outer PTE + inner PTE + actual data). With TLB: on hit = 2 accesses (TLB + data), on miss = 4 accesses.
mustNotMiss: **Direct-mapped:** Tag + Index + Offset bits. Index selects cache set. Tag comparison → hit/miss. Simple but high conflict misses.
**Write-through:** Write to both cache and memory simultaneously. No dirty bit needed. Write-back: write only to cache (dirty bit). Flush to memory on eviction.
**From A3:** AMAT = L1 hit time + L1 miss rate × (L2 hit time + L2 miss rate × MM time). TLB EAT = h×t_m + (1-h)×(t_m+t_p). 2-level: h×t_m + (1-h)×(2×t_p+t_m).

interviewQuestions.question: L1 hit=2ns, L1 miss rate=10%, L2 hit=8ns, L2 miss rate=5%, MM=100ns. Find AMAT.
Virtual address space = 32 bits, page size = 4KB, 2-level page table. How large is each page table?
interviewQuestions.answer: Page size = 4KB = 2^12 → offset = 12 bits. Remaining = 20 bits split 10+10. Each level has 2^10 = 1024 entries × 4 bytes/entry = **4KB per page table**. Elegant: each page table fits exactly in one page.

topics.name: COA 4: I/O Systems & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~10 lectures + DPPs 10–11 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥85%, AMAT < 3 min). I/O: programmed I/O (busy-wait), interrupt-driven I/O, DMA (direct memory access). DMA for large transfers — frees CPU. All 11 COA DPPs completed.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 10–11: Final COA DPPs on paper
Final PYQ: All GATE COA PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥85%, AMAT < 3 min)

learningTip: **Rule 7 (from guide):** All 11 COA DPPs must be on paper. Write AMAT formula before EVERY COA problem. Write pipeline CPI formula. COA = 8–10 marks of ENTIRELY numerical questions.
**DMA cycle stealing:** DMA steals memory bus cycles from CPU. CPU slows slightly but is otherwise free to execute instructions. Far more efficient than interrupt-driven for bulk transfers.
**Gate target: COA PYQ ≥85%, AMAT < 3 min.** Practice AMAT until it's a reflex, not a calculation.
mustNotMiss: **Programmed I/O:** CPU busy-waits (polls) → CPU fully occupied. Simple but wastes CPU cycles.
**Interrupt-driven:** CPU issues I/O command, resumes other work. On completion, device raises interrupt. More efficient but overhead per interrupt.
**DMA:** CPU sets up DMA (source, destination, count). DMA handles entire transfer. Interrupts CPU only on completion. Ideal for disk, network transfers.

interviewQuestions.question: Why is DMA preferred over interrupt-driven I/O for large data transfers?
interviewQuestions.answer: Interrupt-driven: one interrupt per byte/word → thousands of interrupts for a 1MB transfer → huge overhead. **DMA:** one interrupt per transfer, regardless of size. For large transfers (disk reads), DMA reduces interrupt overhead by orders of magnitude, freeing CPU for computation.

interviewQuestions.question: Disk: seek=5ms, rotational latency avg=3ms, transfer rate=50MB/s, transfer=100KB. Find total access time.
interviewQuestions.answer: Seek = 5ms. Avg rotational latency = 3ms (half revolution). Transfer time = 100KB / 50MB/s = 0.1/50 × 1000 = 2ms. Total = 5 + 3 + 2 = **10ms**.

topics.name: DL 1: Boolean Algebra, K-Maps & Combinational Circuits
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPPs 1–2. Boolean algebra axioms, De Morgan's laws, duality, canonical SOP/POS, K-maps (2/3-variable), 4-variable K-maps (corners adjacent, don't-cares → simplify to minimum SOP). Combinational: adders (ripple carry, carry lookahead), MUX as universal element, decoder, priority encoder.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: K-map + combinational circuits on paper
DPP 2: MUX, decoder and logic expression problems

learningTip: **K-map grouping rules:** Groups must be powers of 2 (1,2,4,8,16). Cover all 1s. Maximize group sizes. 4-variable K-map: corners (0,2,8,10) form a valid group!
**Don't-care cells:** Can be used in groups (to maximize group size) or ignored. They give flexibility — always treat them as 1 when they help form a larger group.
**Gate target: K-maps < 2 min.** Practice until you automatically see the optimal grouping without trial and error.
mustNotMiss: **De Morgan's:** NOT(A AND B) = NOT-A OR NOT-B. NOT(A OR B) = NOT-A AND NOT-B. Duality: swap AND↔OR and 0↔1.
**SOP vs POS:** SOP (Sum of Products) → use K-map 1-cells. POS (Product of Sums) → use K-map 0-cells.
**MUX as universal:** A 4:1 MUX can implement any function of 3 variables. 2:1 MUX can implement any function with literals on inputs.

interviewQuestions.question: Minimize F(A,B,C,D) = Σm(0,1,2,4,5,8,9,10) using K-map.
interviewQuestions.answer: K-map: Mark minterms 0,1,2,4,5,8,9,10. Group: {0,1,2,8,9,10} → wait, check adjacency. Groups: {0,1,2,4,5,10,8,9} (review 4-var K-map layout). Best grouping: {0,1,8,9}=A'D', {0,2,8,10}=A'C', {0,4}=... . Final minimal SOP ≈ **B'D' + A'D' + A'C'** (verify with K-map).

interviewQuestions.question: How can a 4:1 MUX implement the function F(A,B,C) = A'B'C + AB'C + ABC?
interviewQuestions.answer: Use A,B as select lines (MSB=A). For each combination of (A,B)=(00,01,10,11), determine F: (0,0):F=C, (0,1):F=0, (1,0):F=C, (1,1):F=1. Connect MUX inputs: I0=C, I1=0, I2=C, I3=1.

topics.name: DL 2: Sequential Circuits, Flip-Flops & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~11 lectures + DPP 3 + Final GATE PYQ test (target ≥84%, K-maps < 2 min). Flip-flop excitation tables (all 4 from memory: SR, JK, D, T), Moore vs Mealy machines, sequential circuit design procedure, state reduction.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 3: Sequential circuit + flip-flop excitation on paper
Final PYQ: All GATE DL PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥84%)

learningTip: **Excitation tables cold (from A3):** SR: 0→0:S=0,R=x · 0→1:S=1,R=0 · 1→0:S=0,R=1 · 1→1:S=x,R=0. JK: 0→0:J=0,K=x · 0→1:J=1,K=x · 1→0:J=x,K=1 · 1→1:J=x,K=0. D: D=Q_next always. T: T=Q⊕Q_next.
**Sequential circuit design steps:** State diagram → State table → State reduction → State assignment → K-map for next state and output → Logic circuit.
**Moore vs Mealy:** Moore: output depends only on current state. Mealy: output depends on current state AND input. Mealy has fewer states but output changes asynchronously with input.
mustNotMiss: **From A3 — flip-flop excitation tables** (must be memorized completely cold): D is simplest (D=Q_next). T toggles on T=1. SR forbids S=R=1. JK is SR with no forbidden state.
**Characteristic equations:** SR: Q_next=S+R'Q (S≠R condition). JK: Q_next=JQ'+K'Q. D: Q_next=D. T: Q_next=T⊕Q.
**State reduction:** Two states are equivalent if (1) same outputs and (2) same next states for all inputs.

interviewQuestions.question: Design a T flip-flop using JK flip-flop.
interviewQuestions.answer: T flip-flop: Q_next = T⊕Q. JK flip-flop: Q_next = JQ'+K'Q. Equate: need JQ'+K'Q = TQ'+T'Q... Wait — T=1 should toggle. If T=1: J=1,K=1 (JK toggles). If T=0: J=0,K=0 (JK holds). So **J=T and K=T**. Connect T to both J and K inputs.

interviewQuestions.question: Difference between Moore and Mealy machines. Which has fewer states?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Moore:** Output = f(current state only). Output changes only at clock edge. More states needed (each output needs its own state). \n**Mealy:** Output = f(current state + input). Output can change asynchronously with input. **Fewer states** — each state can produce different outputs for different inputs.

topics.name: GA 1: Quantitative Reasoning
topics.description: ~6 lectures + DPPs 1–14. Ratio/proportion, percentages, profit-loss, time-speed-distance (D=S×T), time-work, number series, mixtures and alligation. Each DPP is 45 min under exam conditions. Target: 95%+ accuracy on all quantitative DPPs.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 1–10: Quantitative reasoning on paper
DPPs 11–14: Number series and mixture problems

learningTip: **GA first on every mock and the real exam.** 10 questions, 15 marks, typically < 20 min. Locking in 13–15/15 on GA before touching technical questions is a score multiplier.
**Alligation shortcut:** When mixing two solutions of concentrations C1 and C2 to get Cm: ratio = (Cm−C2):(C1−Cm). Draw cross-diagram.
**Speed-distance-work pattern:** In GATE GA, most numerical questions reduce to D=S×T or Work=(Rate×Time). Identify the invariant (total distance, total work) and solve.
mustNotMiss: **Profit-loss formulas:** SP = CP×(1+P/100). Discount: MP×(1-D/100)=SP. Profit%=(SP-CP)/CP×100.
**Compound interest:** A = P(1+r/n)^(nt). For GATE: usually n=1 (annual) or n=2 (half-yearly).
**Gate target: DPP accuracy ≥95%.** GA is 15 marks. Missing even 3 GA questions = -6 marks on relative ranking (vs peers who score near-perfect on GA).

interviewQuestions.question: A train 200m long crosses a 400m platform in 30s. Find the speed of the train.
interviewQuestions.answer: Total distance = train length + platform = 200+400 = 600m. Time = 30s. Speed = 600/30 = **20 m/s = 72 km/h**.

interviewQuestions.question: A mixture of milk and water is 4:1. 10L is removed and replaced with water. New ratio = 3:2. Find original quantity.

topics.name: GA 2: Analytical, Verbal & Final PYQ
topics.description: ~6 lectures + DPPs 15–20 + Final GATE GA PYQ test (target ≥95%). Analytical: syllogisms (3-circle Venn diagrams), critical reasoning, blood relations, directions. Verbal: sentence correction, reading comprehension, vocabulary-in-context.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 15–17: Analytical reasoning problems
Final PYQ: All GATE GA PYQs 2015–2026 (target ≥95%)

learningTip: **Syllogism method:** Draw Venn diagrams. Mark definite inclusions as filled regions. Test each conclusion by checking if it's TRUE in ALL valid Venn diagrams. If any valid diagram makes it false → conclusion does NOT follow.
**Blood relations shortcut:** Convert statements to a tree diagram. Identify gender from context. Label each node. Then trace the chain from the first person to the last.
**Sentence correction:** Look for subject-verb agreement, tense consistency, pronoun reference, and parallel structure. GATE verbal questions are typically straightforward.
mustNotMiss: **Directions:** Use compass: North=up, East=right. After a series of turns, draw on paper. Final direction and distance from start are what GA tests.
**Reading comprehension:** Read question BEFORE passage. Locate answer in passage directly — avoid inference unless the question asks for it.
**DPP accuracy ≥95% gate condition.** 20 DPPs across all GA types — all must be done on paper under 45-minute timed conditions.

interviewQuestions.question: All A are B. Some B are C. Conclusion: Some A are C — valid?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Not necessarily valid.** Draw Venn: A fully inside B. C overlaps part of B but may not overlap A. There exists a valid diagram where A and C don't overlap. So 'Some A are C' does **NOT** follow with certainty.

interviewQuestions.question: A man walks 5km North, turns right 3km, turns right 4km. What is his distance from starting point?
interviewQuestions.answer: Start (0,0). North 5km → (0,5). Right (East) 3km → (3,5). Right (South) 4km → (3,1). Distance = √((3-0)²+(1-0)²) = √(9+1) = √10 ≈ **3.16 km**.

topics.name: DM 1: Set Theory & Algebraic Structures
topics.description: DM L1–L24 (24 lectures × 2 hr) + DPPs 1–5 (Sun B2, 2.5 hr each). Sets: types, power set, all 5 operations. Relations: 6 types (reflexive/symmetric/antisymmetric/transitive/equivalence/partial order). Functions: injective/surjective/bijective, counting f∘g. Algebraic structures: semigroup → monoid → group → abelian group, Lagrange's theorem, lattices (Hasse diagrams), rings, integral domains, fields. Recurrences: characteristic equation method. Modular arithmetic, CRT, Fermat's little theorem.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 1–2: Sets + Relations (Sun B2, Wk 2)
DPPs 3–5: Functions + Algebraic Structures + Induction/Recurrences (Wk 4, Wk 5)

learningTip: **Group axioms cold:** Closure, Associativity, Identity (e exists s.t. a*e=a), Inverse (a⁻¹ exists). Abelian adds Commutativity.
**Lagrange's theorem:** If H is a subgroup of G, then |H| divides |G|. Used to prove order of elements divides group order.
**Recurrence (characteristic equation):** Write T(n)=aT(n-1)+bT(n-2). Characteristic equation: r²=ar+b. Roots r1,r2 → T(n) = A·r1ⁿ + B·r2ⁿ. Determine A,B from initial conditions.
mustNotMiss: **Counting functions:** Total functions from A(|A|=m) to B(|B|=n): n^m. Injections: P(n,m)=n!/(n-m)!. Bijections: n! (only if m=n).
**Equivalence relation:** Reflexive + Symmetric + Transitive. Partitions the set into equivalence classes.
**Partial order (POSET):** Reflexive + Antisymmetric + Transitive. Hasse diagram removes transitive and reflexive edges.

interviewQuestions.question: Prove that in any group G, if a² = e for all a ∈ G, then G is abelian.
interviewQuestions.answer: For any a,b ∈ G: (ab)² = e → abab = e → ab = b⁻¹a⁻¹. But a²=e → a⁻¹=a and b⁻¹=b. So ab = ba. **G is abelian.** ✓

interviewQuestions.question: Solve T(n) = 3T(n-1) - 2T(n-2), T(0)=1, T(1)=3.
interviewQuestions.answer: Characteristic equation: r² - 3r + 2 = 0 → (r-1)(r-2) = 0. Roots: r₁=1, r₂=2. General solution: T(n) = A·1ⁿ + B·2ⁿ. T(0)=A+B=1. T(1)=A+2B=3 → B=2, A=-1. **T(n) = -1 + 2^(n+1)**.

topics.name: DM 2: Graph Theory
topics.description: DM L25–L36 = Ch02 L1–L12 (12 lectures × 2 hr) + DPPs 6–8 (Sun B2, 2.5 hr each). Graphs: definitions, handshaking lemma, paths/cycles/connectivity, trees (n-1 edges, Cayley's formula n^(n-2)), BFS/DFS, Eulerian/Hamiltonian, bipartite (König's theorem), planar (Euler's formula V-E+F=2, K5/K3,3 non-planar), graph coloring, matchings, MST.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 6–7: Graph basics + Trees (Sun B2, Wk 6)
DPP 8: Full graph theory PYQ (Sun B2, Wk 7)

learningTip: **Euler's formula for planar graphs:** V - E + F = 2 (connected planar graph). For trees: F=1 → V-E+1=2 → V=E+1 ✓.
**Eulerian circuit condition (undirected):** Graph is connected + ALL vertices have even degree. Eulerian path: exactly 2 vertices have odd degree.
**König's theorem (bipartite):** Max matching = Min vertex cover. Useful for GATE optimization questions.
mustNotMiss: **Handshaking Lemma:** Σdeg(v) = 2|E|. Sum of all degrees is even. Odd-degree vertices always come in pairs.
**Cayley's formula:** Number of labeled trees on n vertices = n^(n-2). For n=4: 4^2=16 labeled trees.
**Chromatic number:** χ(tree)=2, χ(odd cycle)=3, χ(Kₙ)=n. Planar graph: χ ≤ 4 (4-colour theorem).

interviewQuestions.question: A connected planar graph has 10 vertices and 15 edges. How many faces does it have?
interviewQuestions.answer: Euler's formula: V - E + F = 2. 10 - 15 + F = 2. **F = 7 faces.**

interviewQuestions.question: Prove K5 is non-planar.
interviewQuestions.answer: K5: V=5, E=10. For planar graph with no triangles: E ≤ 2V-4 → 2×5-4=6. But E=10 > 6. Wait — K5 HAS triangles, so use E ≤ 3V-6 → 3×5-6=9. E=10 > 9. **Contradiction → K5 is non-planar.** (Or use Kuratowski's theorem directly.)

topics.name: DM 3: Propositional & Predicate Logic
topics.description: DM L37–L44 = Ch03 L1–L8 (8 lectures × 2 hr) + DPPs 9–10 (Sun B2, 2.5 hr each). Propositional logic: connectives, truth tables, tautology, CNF/DNF conversion (3 methods), resolution. Predicate logic: ∀, ∃, nested quantifiers, negation rules (push ¬ inward). Prenex Normal Form. Inference rules: modus ponens, modus tollens, hypothetical syllogism. Formal proofs.

practiceQuestions.name: DPPs 9–10: Logic PYQs (propositional + predicate) (Wk 8)
GATE PYQ: Predicate logic and inference

learningTip: **Negation of quantifiers:** ¬(∀x P(x)) = ∃x ¬P(x). ¬(∃x P(x)) = ∀x ¬P(x). For nested: push ¬ inward one quantifier at a time, flipping each.
**CNF conversion:** (1) Eliminate ↔ and →. (2) Push ¬ inward (De Morgan). (3) Distribute ∨ over ∧.
**Resolution rule:** From (A ∨ B) and (¬A ∨ C), derive (B ∨ C). Used in automated theorem proving. To prove F, add ¬F and derive contradiction (empty clause).
mustNotMiss: **Tautology:** True for ALL truth assignments. **Contradiction:** False for all. **Satisfiable:** True for SOME assignment.
**Modus Ponens:** P, P→Q ⊢ Q. **Modus Tollens:** ¬Q, P→Q ⊢ ¬P. Know these cold — GATE inference questions use them directly.
**FOL validity is undecidable** (Church-Turing). Propositional logic satisfiability is NP-complete (SAT).

interviewQuestions.question: Negate the statement: ∀x ∃y (P(x,y) → Q(x,y)).
interviewQuestions.answer: ¬(∀x ∃y (P→Q)) = ∃x ∀y ¬(P→Q) = ∃x ∀y ¬(¬P∨Q) = **∃x ∀y (P(x,y) ∧ ¬Q(x,y))**. There exists an x such that for all y, P(x,y) holds but Q(x,y) does not.

interviewQuestions.question: Convert (P → Q) ↔ R to CNF.
interviewQuestions.answer: Step 1: Eliminate ↔: ((P→Q)→R) ∧ (R→(P→Q)). Step 2: Eliminate →: (¬(¬P∨Q)∨R) ∧ (¬R∨(¬P∨Q)). Step 3: Simplify: ((P∧¬Q)∨R) ∧ (¬R∨¬P∨Q). Step 4: Distribute first clause: (P∨R)∧(¬Q∨R). CNF= **(P∨R) ∧ (¬Q∨R) ∧ (¬R∨¬P∨Q)**.

topics.name: DM 4: Combinatorics & Assessment
topics.description: DM L45–L47 = Ch04 L1–L3 (3 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 11 (Sun B2, 2.5 hr) + DM Assessment (Fri, 2 hr slot). Pigeonhole principle (basic + generalized), inclusion-exclusion |A∪B∪C|, derangements Dn = n!·Σ(-1)^k/k!, circular permutations (n-1)!, multinomial coefficients, stars-and-bars distribution.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 11: Combinatorics (Wk 9 Sun B2)
DM Assessment: 30 Qs all chapters, 45 min (target ≥25/30)

learningTip: **Generalized pigeonhole:** n items in k boxes → at least one box has ≥ ⌈n/k⌉ items. Make sure to use ceiling, not floor.
**Stars and bars:** Distributing n identical items into k distinct bins with no restriction: C(n+k-1, k-1).
mustNotMiss: **Inclusion-Exclusion:** |A∪B∪C| = |A|+|B|+|C| - |A∩B| - |A∩C| - |B∩C| + |A∩B∩C|.
**DM Assessment gate condition:** ≥25/30. Score 21–24: repair Wk 10. Below 21: repair all Wk 10. DM underpins TOC, Algorithms, and Probability.
**Circular permutations:** n distinct objects in a circle = (n-1)!. With reflection symmetry (necklace): (n-1)!/2.

interviewQuestions.question: In a group of 367 people, must at least 2 share a birthday? Why?
interviewQuestions.answer: 365 possible birthdays (ignore leap year). 367 people in 365 pigeonholes. By **Pigeonhole Principle**: ⌈367/365⌉ = 2. At least one 'box' (birthday) has ≥2 people. **Yes, guaranteed.**

interviewQuestions.question: How many ways can 5 people sit around a circular table, with 2 specific people never adjacent?
interviewQuestions.answer: Total circular arrangements = (5-1)! = 24. Arrangements where A,B ARE adjacent: treat A,B as one unit → 4 units in circle = (4-1)! × 2 = 12. NOT adjacent = 24 - 12 = **12 arrangements**.

topics.name: LA 1: Vector Spaces, Rank & Linear Systems
topics.description: LA L1–L10 (10 lectures × 2 hr). Vector spaces: subspaces, span, linear independence, basis, dimension. Row reduction: REF, RREF, rank via elementary row operations. Null space, column space, rank-nullity theorem: rank(A) + nullity(A) = n. Linear transformations: kernel, image, matrix representation. Systems: consistent (unique if rank=n; infinite if rank<n) vs inconsistent (rank(A)≠rank([A|b])).

practiceQuestions.name: GATE PYQ: Rank and null space problems
GATE PYQ: System of equations consistency

learningTip: **Rank-Nullity theorem:** rank(A) + nullity(A) = number of columns of A (n). Nullity = dimension of null space = number of free variables in RREF.
**System consistency check:** Augmented matrix [A|b]. If rank(A) ≠ rank([A|b]) → inconsistent. If rank(A) = rank([A|b]) = n → unique solution. If rank < n → infinite solutions.
**Linear independence check:** Row-reduce the matrix. Vectors are independent iff no row becomes all zeros in RREF.
mustNotMiss: **Rank = number of non-zero rows in REF = number of pivot columns.**
**Null space (kernel):** Set of all x where Ax=0. Dimension = nullity. Found by solving RREF(A)x=0.
**Column space (range/image):** Span of column vectors. Dimension = rank. For Ax=b to be consistent, b must be in the column space.

interviewQuestions.question: Matrix A is 3×5. rank(A)=2. Find dim(null space).
interviewQuestions.answer: Rank-Nullity: rank + nullity = n (columns) = 5. **nullity = 5 - 2 = 3.** The null space has dimension 3.

interviewQuestions.question: How do you determine if the system Ax=b has a solution without solving it?
interviewQuestions.answer: Check if rank(A) = rank([A|b]). If yes, solution exists (consistent). If rank(A) < rank([A|b]), the vector b is NOT in the column space of A → **no solution (inconsistent).** Equivalently: b must be a linear combination of columns of A.

topics.name: LA 2: Eigenvalues, Diagonalization & Assessment
topics.description: LA L11–L14 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + LA Assessment (Fri, 2 hr slot: 20 GATE PYQs, 40 min, target ≥75%). Eigenvalues via characteristic polynomial det(A−λI)=0. Eigenvectors and eigenspaces. Algebraic multiplicity (AM) ≥ Geometric multiplicity (GM). Diagonalization: A=PDP⁻¹ (possible iff AM=GM for all eigenvalues). Orthogonality: Gram-Schmidt, QR decomposition. Positive definite: all eigenvalues > 0.

practiceQuestions.name: LA Assessment: 20 GATE PYQs in 40 min (target ≥75%)
GATE PYQ: Diagonalization conditions

learningTip: **AM vs GM:** Algebraic multiplicity = root multiplicity in characteristic polynomial. Geometric multiplicity = dim(eigenspace) = n - rank(A-λI). Always AM ≥ GM.
**Diagonalizable iff:** For EVERY eigenvalue, AM = GM. Symmetric matrices are always diagonalizable (real eigenvalues, orthogonal eigenvectors).
**Positive definite tests:** All eigenvalues > 0. OR all leading principal minors > 0 (Sylvester's criterion).
mustNotMiss: **Characteristic polynomial:** det(A−λI) = 0. For 2×2: λ² - trace(A)·λ + det(A) = 0. Use this shortcut.
**Similar matrices** have the same eigenvalues. A and P⁻¹AP have the same characteristic polynomial.
**Trace = sum of eigenvalues. Determinant = product of eigenvalues.** Verify after computing eigenvalues.

interviewQuestions.question: A = [[3,1],[0,3]]. Is A diagonalizable?
interviewQuestions.answer: Characteristic polynomial: (3-λ)² = 0 → λ=3 (AM=2). Eigenspace: (A-3I)x=0 → [[0,1],[0,0]]x=0 → x2=0, x1 free → eigenspace dim=1. GM=1 < AM=2. **A is NOT diagonalizable** (defective matrix).

interviewQuestions.question: Find eigenvalues of A = [[2,3],[1,4]].
interviewQuestions.answer: det(A-λI) = (2-λ)(4-λ)-3 = λ²-6λ+8-3 = λ²-6λ+5 = (λ-1)(λ-5) = 0. **Eigenvalues: λ₁=1, λ₂=5.** Verify: trace=6=1+5 ✓, det=5=1×5 ✓.

topics.name: Calculus 1: Limits & Derivatives
topics.description: Calc L1–L4 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + PYQ Free Day 1 (2 hr, 10 PYQs on limits + derivatives). Limits: ε-δ definition, L'Hôpital's rule (0/0 and ∞/∞), standard limits. Continuity: IVT, removable/jump/infinite discontinuities. Derivatives: chain/product/quotient/implicit. MVT (f'(c)=(f(b)-f(a))/(b-a)). Rolle's theorem.

practiceQuestions.name: PYQ Free Day 1: 10 limits + derivatives PYQs (Wk 13 Fri)

learningTip: **L'Hôpital's rule:** If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, then lim f(x)/g(x) = lim f'(x)/g'(x). Apply repeatedly if needed. Only applies to 0/0 or ∞/∞ forms.
**MVT critical application:** If f is differentiable on (a,b) and continuous on [a,b], there exists c ∈ (a,b) where f'(c) = (f(b)-f(a))/(b-a). Use this to prove existence of zeros of derivatives.
mustNotMiss: **Standard limits:** lim(x→0) sin(x)/x = 1. lim(x→0) (1+x)^(1/x) = e. lim(x→∞) (1+1/x)^x = e.
**Differentiability implies continuity.** Continuity does NOT imply differentiability. |x| is continuous at 0 but not differentiable.
**Rolle's theorem:** f(a)=f(b) and continuous → ∃c with f'(c)=0. Special case of MVT.

interviewQuestions.question: Find lim(x→0) (e^x - 1 - x) / x².
interviewQuestions.answer: 0/0 form. L'Hôpital: (e^x - 1) / (2x). Still 0/0. L'Hôpital again: e^x / 2. At x=0: **1/2**.

interviewQuestions.question: Is f(x) = x^(1/3) differentiable at x=0?
interviewQuestions.answer: f'(0) = lim(h→0) (h^(1/3) - 0)/h = lim h^(1/3)/h = lim h^(-2/3). As h→0, h^(-2/3) → ∞. **NOT differentiable at x=0** (vertical tangent). But f is continuous at 0.

topics.name: Calculus 2: Integration
topics.description: Calc L5–L8 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + PYQ Free Day 2 (2 hr, 10 PYQs on integration). Integration by substitution, integration by parts (∫u dv = uv − ∫v du). Definite integral: FTC Part 1 (∫a to x f(t)dt differentiable) and Part 2 (∫a to b f(x)dx = F(b)-F(a)). Area between curves.

practiceQuestions.name: PYQ Free Day 2: 10 integration PYQs (Wk 14 Fri)

learningTip: **Integration by parts mnemonic (LIATE):** Choose u as the Logarithm, Inverse trig, Algebraic, Trigonometric, Exponential — in order. u = first type encountered in LIATE.
**FTC Part 1:** d/dx [∫a to x f(t)dt] = f(x). The derivative of the integral returns the original function.
mustNotMiss: **FTC Part 2:** ∫a to b f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a) where F'=f. The fundamental bridge between differentiation and integration.
**Standard integrals:** ∫e^x dx = e^x. ∫1/x dx = ln|x|. ∫sin(x) dx = -cos(x). ∫x^n dx = x^(n+1)/(n+1) for n≠-1.

interviewQuestions.question: Evaluate ∫x·e^x dx.
interviewQuestions.answer: Integration by parts. u=x, dv=e^x dx → du=dx, v=e^x. ∫x·e^x dx = x·e^x - ∫e^x dx = **x·e^x - e^x + C = e^x(x-1) + C.**

interviewQuestions.question: Find d/dx [∫1 to x² sin(t²) dt].
interviewQuestions.answer: Chain rule + FTC Part 1. Let F(x) = ∫1 to x sin(t²)dt. Then our integral = F(x²). d/dx[F(x²)] = F'(x²)·2x = **sin(x⁴)·2x.**

topics.name: Calculus 3: Multivariable & Series
topics.description: Calc L9–L12 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + PYQ Free Day 3 (2 hr, 10 PYQs on series + multivariable). Partial derivatives, gradient ∇f, directional derivative. Total derivative, Jacobian, multivariable chain rule. Double integrals (iterated, change of variables). Taylor series: Maclaurin for e^x, sin x, cos x, ln(1+x), (1+x)^n. Radius of convergence.

practiceQuestions.name: PYQ Free Day 3: 10 series + multivariable PYQs (Wk 15 Fri)

learningTip: **Maclaurin series (memorize all 5 from A3):** e^x = Σxⁿ/n!. sin(x) = x - x³/3! + x⁵/5! ... cos(x) = 1 - x²/2! + x⁴/4! ... ln(1+x) = x - x²/2 + x³/3 ... (1+x)^n = 1 + nx + n(n-1)x²/2! ...
**Directional derivative:** D_u f = ∇f · u where u is a unit vector. Maximum directional derivative = |∇f| in direction of ∇f.
mustNotMiss: **Gradient ∇f = (∂f/∂x, ∂f/∂y, ∂f/∂z).** Points in direction of steepest ascent. Magnitude = rate of fastest increase.
**Critical points of f(x,y):** Set ∂f/∂x=0 and ∂f/∂y=0. Then use second derivative test (Hessian determinant) to classify.

interviewQuestions.question: Find the Maclaurin series for e^(-x²) and use it to find ∫₀¹ e^(-x²) dx.

topics.name: Calculus 4: Advanced Topics & Assessment
topics.description: Calc L13–L16 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + Calculus Assessment (Fri, 2 hr slot: 20 PYQs, 40 min, target ≥72%). Advanced integration techniques, convergence tests for series, applications. Practice all GATE calculus questions types.

practiceQuestions.name: Calculus Assessment: 20 PYQs in 40 min (target ≥72%)

learningTip: **Convergence tests:** Ratio test: lim |a(n+1)/a(n)| < 1 → converges. Comparison test. Alternating series test. Know when to apply each.
**Gate target: Calculus PYQ ≥72%.** Combined Eng Math Assessment (LA+Calc+P+Stats) target ≥33/40.
mustNotMiss: **Ratio test:** L = lim |aₙ₊₁/aₙ|. L<1 → converges absolutely. L>1 → diverges. L=1 → inconclusive.
**Improper integrals:** ∫1 to ∞ 1/x^p dx converges iff p > 1. ∫0 to 1 1/x^p dx converges iff p < 1.

interviewQuestions.question: Does Σ n/2^n converge? Apply the ratio test.
interviewQuestions.answer: a(n) = n/2^n. a(n+1) = (n+1)/2^(n+1). Ratio = (n+1)/2n. As n→∞: limit = 1/2 < 1. **Converges** by ratio test. (Sum = 2)

topics.name: P+Stats 1: Probability Basics & Bayes' Theorem
topics.description: Prob L1–L4 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + PYQ Free Day 1 (2 hr, 10 Bayes' theorem PYQs). Sample space, events, Kolmogorov axioms, classical probability, conditional probability P(A|B)=P(A∩B)/P(B), independent events, multiplication rule, Bayes' theorem with probability trees.

practiceQuestions.name: PYQ Free Day 1: 10 Bayes' theorem PYQs (Wk 17 Fri)

learningTip: **From A3 — Bayes' theorem:** P(A|B) = P(B|A)×P(A) / P(B). **Draw a probability tree for EVERY Bayes' problem.** Never use the formula without the tree.
**Total probability:** P(B) = Σ P(B|Aᵢ)×P(Aᵢ) over all partitions {Aᵢ}.
mustNotMiss: **Independent vs mutually exclusive:** Independent: P(A∩B)=P(A)×P(B). Mutually exclusive: P(A∩B)=0. Independent events CAN co-occur; mutually exclusive events CANNOT.
**Bayes' theorem:** P(A|B) = P(B|A)·P(A) / [P(B|A)·P(A) + P(B|A')·P(A')].

interviewQuestions.question: A test for a disease has 99% sensitivity (true positive) and 95% specificity. Disease prevalence = 1%. Patient tests positive. P(disease | positive test)?

topics.name: P+Stats 2: Probability Distributions
topics.description: Prob L5–L8 (4 lectures × 2 hr) + PYQ Free Day 2 (2 hr, 10 PYQs on Binomial/Poisson/Normal). PMF, CDF, E[X], Var(X)=E[X²]-(E[X])². MGF. Binomial B(n,p): mean=np, var=np(1-p). Poisson Pois(λ): mean=var=λ. Normal N(μ,σ²): 68-95-99.7 rule. Exponential (memoryless property). Uniform U(a,b).

practiceQuestions.name: PYQ Free Day 2: 10 distribution PYQs (Wk 18 Fri)

learningTip: **Binomial → Poisson approximation:** When n is large and p is small, B(n,p) ≈ Pois(λ=np). Good approximation when n≥20 and p≤0.05.
**Memoryless property:** P(X>s+t | X>s) = P(X>t) for Exponential distribution. Only continuous memoryless distribution. Geometric is discrete memoryless.
mustNotMiss: **Var(X) = E[X²] - (E[X])².** Also: Var(aX+b) = a²Var(X). Var(X+Y) = Var(X)+Var(Y) iff X,Y independent.
**Exponential (from guide):** Memoryless: P(X>s+t|X>s)=P(X>t). Mean=1/λ, Var=1/λ².

interviewQuestions.question: X~Poisson(4). Find P(X=2).

topics.name: P+Stats 3: Joint Distributions, Markov Chains & Combined Assessment
topics.description: Prob L9–L22 (14 lectures × 2 hr) + 2 free-day PYQ sessions + Combined Eng Math Assessment (Thu, 40 Qs, 60 min: LA+Calc+Prob+Stats, target ≥33/40). Joint distributions: marginals, conditional pdfs, independence. Covariance, correlation ρ. Functions of RVs. LLN, CLT. Markov chains: transition matrix, n-step, steady-state π.

practiceQuestions.name: Combined Eng Math Assessment: 40 Qs LA+Calc+Prob, 60 min (target ≥33/40)
GATE PYQ: Markov chains and steady state

learningTip: **Steady-state Markov (from A3):** Solve πP=π, Σπᵢ=1. For 2-state chain with P=[[1-a,a],[b,1-b]]: π₁=b/(a+b), π₂=a/(a+b).
**CLT (from A3):** √n(X̄-μ)/σ → N(0,1) as n→∞. X̄ = sample mean of n i.i.d. samples. Powerful because distribution of X doesn't matter.
**Eng Math gate condition:** ≥33/40. Score 29–32: repair Mon–Wed before TOC begins. This is the highest-stakes assessment in Phase 1+2.
mustNotMiss: **Cov(X,Y) = E[XY] - E[X]E[Y].** Independent → uncorrelated (Cov=0). Uncorrelated does NOT imply independent.
**LLN:** X̄ → μ as n→∞ (in probability). Sample mean converges to population mean.
**Markov property:** P(Xₙ₊₁|X₁,...,Xₙ) = P(Xₙ₊₁|Xₙ). Past is irrelevant given present state.

interviewQuestions.question: 2-state Markov chain: P = [[0.7, 0.3],[0.4, 0.6]]. Find steady-state distribution.
interviewQuestions.answer: Solve π₁+π₂=1 and πP=π. 0.7π₁+0.4π₂=π₁ → 0.4π₂=0.3π₁ → π₁/π₂=4/3. With π₁+π₂=1: **π₁=4/7≈0.571, π₂=3/7≈0.429**.

interviewQuestions.question: n=64 samples drawn from a distribution with μ=50, σ=16. What is P(X̄ > 53)?

topics.name: TOC 1: DFA, NFA & Regular Languages
topics.description: TOC L1–L7 (7 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 1 (Fri, 2 hr slot: DFA/NFA construction on paper) + PYQ Free Day 1 (2 hr, 25 PYQs). DFA: formal 5-tuple (Q,Σ,δ,q0,F), δ*. DFA construction: even 0s, divisibility by 3. DFA minimization: table-filling (Myhill-Nerode). NFA: ε-transitions, ε-closure, NFA→DFA subset construction. Regular Expressions: Kleene's theorem (RE=NFA=DFA). RL closure properties.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1: DFA/NFA construction on paper (Wk 23 Fri)
PYQ Free Day 1: 25 GATE DFA/NFA/CFG PYQs (Wk 25 Fri)

learningTip: **DFA construction rule:** Build state table systematically. Start: What does each state 'remember'? E.g., divisibility by 3: states represent remainder (0,1,2). δ(0,0)=0, δ(0,1)=1, δ(1,0)=2, δ(1,1)=0, δ(2,0)=1, δ(2,1)=2.
**Gate target: DFA construction < 3 min.** Every DFA must be constructed fully — not approximated. Draw the diagram, all transitions, acceptance states.
mustNotMiss: **RL closure:** Closed under union, concatenation, Kleene star, complement, intersection, reversal. NOT closed under... (all closed — RL is closed under all boolean ops).
**DFA minimization:** Two states are distinguishable if one is accepting and other is not, OR their transitions on some symbol lead to distinguishable states (table filling algorithm).
**NFA→DFA (subset construction):** States of DFA = subsets of NFA states. Can cause exponential blowup in worst case but NFA and DFA accept same languages.

interviewQuestions.question: Design a DFA for strings over {0,1} with even number of 0s and even number of 1s.
interviewQuestions.answer: States encode (parity of 0s, parity of 1s): q00(ee), q01(eo), q10(oe), q11(oo). Start+Accept: q00. Transitions: On 0, toggle first component. On 1, toggle second. δ(q00,0)=q10, δ(q00,1)=q01, δ(q10,0)=q00, δ(q10,1)=q11. 4-state DFA.

interviewQuestions.question: Are Regular Languages closed under complement? Prove.
interviewQuestions.answer: Yes. Given DFA M accepting L, construct M' by swapping accepting and non-accepting states. M' accepts exactly Σ* - L = complement of L. RLs are closed under complement because DFAs have total transition functions — every string has a definite accept/reject state.

topics.name: TOC 2: CFGs, PDAs & Turing Machines
topics.description: TOC L8–L20 (13 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 2 (Fri, 2 hr: Pumping Lemma + TM) + PYQ Free Day 1 included above. CFG: 4-tuple, derivations, parse trees, ambiguity. CNF and GNF transformations. CYK algorithm O(n³). PDA: 6-tuple, acceptance modes (final state = empty stack). CFL Pumping Lemma: uvwxy. CFL closure properties. TM: 7-tuple, variants (multi-tape, NDTM). Church-Turing thesis.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 2: Pumping Lemma + TM problems (Wk 26 Fri)
GATE PYQ: CFG ambiguity and CFL closure

learningTip: **CFL NOT closed under intersection and complement.** CFL ∩ CFL may not be CFL. CFL ∩ Regular Language IS CFL. This is a classic GATE trap.

**Pumping Lemma for CFLs:** For any CFL L, ∃p s.t. any string z∈L with |z|≥p can be written as z=uvwxy where (1)|vwx|≤p, (2)|vy|≥1, (3)∀i≥0, uv^iwy^ix∈L. To prove NOT-CFL: assume CFL, find a string that can't be pumped.
**DPDA vs NPDA:** DPDA accepts DCFLs. NPDA accepts all CFLs. L = {ww^R} (palindromes) is CFL (NPDA) but not DCFL (no DPDA).
mustNotMiss: **CFL closure:** Closed under union (S→S1|S2), concatenation (S→S1S2), Kleene star. NOT under intersection or complement.
**TM 7-tuple:** (Q, Σ, Γ, δ, q0, q_accept, q_reject). Γ includes blank (⊔). δ: Q×Γ → Q×Γ×{L,R}.
**L={aⁿbⁿcⁿ} is NOT CFL.** Pumping Lemma proof: take s=aᵖbᵖcᵖ. Any vxy spans at most 2 types — pumping disturbs the count balance of the third type.

interviewQuestions.question: Is L = {aⁿbⁿ | n≥1} ∩ {aⁿbⁿ | n is prime} context-free?
interviewQuestions.answer: L = {aⁿbⁿ | n is prime}. This is the intersection of a CFL with a non-CFL. The result is NOT necessarily a CFL. In fact, {aⁿbⁿ | n is prime} is **not context-free** (provable via Pumping Lemma — primes can't be pumped arithmetically).

interviewQuestions.question: Why does a PDA use a stack while a TM uses a tape?
interviewQuestions.answer: Stack = LIFO access only → can match ONE level of nesting (parentheses, aⁿbⁿ). **Tape = random access** → can simulate any computation (Turing-complete). PDA handles CFLs; TM handles recursively enumerable languages. The stack's LIFO limitation is what creates the 'context-free' boundary.

topics.name: TOC 3: Decidability, P/NP & Assessment
topics.description: TOC L21–L29 (9 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 3 (Fri, 2 hr: Decidability + P/NP) + 2 PYQ free days + TOC Assessment (Fri, 2 hr: 25 PYQs, 45 min, target ≥80%). Halting problem (undecidable, diagonalization proof). Rice's theorem. PCP. P, NP, NP-hard, NP-complete. Cook's theorem. NP chain from memory.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 3: Decidability + P/NP (Wk 27 Fri)
TOC Assessment: 25 PYQs in 45 min (target ≥80%, DFA <3 min, NP chain cold)

learningTip: **From A3 — NP chain (cold):** SAT → 3-SAT → Vertex Cover → Independent Set → Clique → Hamiltonian Path → TSP. Each ≤p the next (polynomial-time reduction). Know direction: we reduce FROM a known NP-complete TO the new problem.

**Rice's theorem:** Any non-trivial semantic property of TM languages is undecidable. 'Non-trivial' = not all TMs have it and not no TMs have it. Use to quickly prove undecidability without direct construction.
**TOC gate target: PYQ ≥80%, DFA construction < 3 min.** TOC is abstract — practice is the only path.
mustNotMiss: **Halting problem (HP) is undecidable:** Proof by diagonalization. If HP were decidable by H, construct D that: if H says accepts → loop, if H says loops → accept. Run D on itself → contradiction.

**R vs RE:** R (Recursive/Decidable): TM always halts (accept or reject). RE (Recursively Enumerable): TM accepts members but may loop on non-members. HP is RE but not R. Complement of HP is not even RE.
**NP-complete = NP ∩ NP-hard.** To prove NP-complete: (1) show in NP (verify in poly time). (2) Show NP-hard (reduce known NP-complete ≤p new problem).

interviewQuestions.question: Is the problem 'Does TM M accept at least one string?' decidable?
interviewQuestions.answer: Non-trivial semantic property of TM language (some TMs accept something, some accept nothing). By **Rice's theorem, it is UNDECIDABLE.** (Specifically, it's RE but not R.)

interviewQuestions.question: Explain the NP-chain reduction from SAT to 3-SAT.
interviewQuestions.answer: SAT: given Boolean formula, does any assignment satisfy it? 3-SAT: each clause has exactly 3 literals. **SAT ≤p 3-SAT:** Convert each SAT clause to 3-CNF: (a) 1-literal clause (l): (l∨y∨z)∧(l∨y∨¬z)∧(l∨¬y∨z)∧(l∨¬y∨¬z). (b) 2-literal: add dummy variable. (c) k-literal (k>3): chain with k-3 new variables. This shows 3-SAT is at least as hard as SAT.

topics.name: Compiler 1: Lexical Analysis & Syntax Parsing
topics.description: CL1–CL13 (13 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 1 (Sat B3, 2.5 hr) + Free Day 1 (2 hr: FIRST/FOLLOW + SLR practice). 6 compiler phases. Lexical: tokens, lexemes, RE for tokens, LEX. FIRST/FOLLOW computation. LL(1) parsing conditions and parse tables. LR(0), SLR, LALR(1), CLR(1). Gate target: FIRST/FOLLOW < 8 min, SLR zero errors.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 1 (Sat B3): FIRST/FOLLOW + SLR practice on 2 grammars
Free Day 1: FIRST/FOLLOW + SLR on 2 new grammars (target: < 8 min)

learningTip: **FIRST computation:** FIRST(a) = {a} for terminal. FIRST(ε) = {ε}. FIRST(AB) = FIRST(A). If ε∈FIRST(A), add FIRST(B). Propagate until no change.
**FOLLOW computation:** FOLLOW(S)={$}. For A→αBβ: FOLLOW(B) ⊇ FIRST(β)-{ε}. If ε∈FIRST(β) or β=ε: FOLLOW(B) ⊇ FOLLOW(A). Propagate until stable.
**LL(1) conditions (from A3):** For each A→α|β, FIRST(α)∩FIRST(β)=∅. If ε∈FIRST(α) then FIRST(α-ε)∩FOLLOW(A)=∅.
mustNotMiss: **SLR vs CLR vs LALR power:** LR(0) ⊂ SLR ⊂ LALR(1) ⊂ CLR(1). LALR(1) is most commonly used (yacc/bison). CLR(1) is most powerful but has more states.
**Shift-reduce conflict:** A state can both shift on token t OR reduce — usually due to ambiguous grammar. Reduce-reduce conflict: two rules want to reduce — grammar design error.
**Gate condition:** FIRST/FOLLOW < 8 min, SLR zero errors, CL21 (Live Variable Analysis) NOT SKIPPED.
