interviewQuestions.question: Compute FIRST and FOLLOW for: S → AB, A → aA | ε, B → b.
interviewQuestions.answer: FIRST(A) = {a, ε}. FIRST(B) = {b}. FIRST(S) = FIRST(A) ∪ FIRST(B) [since ε∈FIRST(A)] = {a, b}. FOLLOW(S) = {$}. FOLLOW(A): S→AB → add FIRST(B)={b} → FOLLOW(A)={b}. FOLLOW(B): S→AB → add FOLLOW(S)={$} → **FOLLOW(B)={$}.**

interviewQuestions.question: Why is LALR(1) preferred over CLR(1) in practice?
interviewQuestions.answer: **CLR(1):** Separate state for each distinct lookahead → many states → large parse tables, more memory. **LALR(1):** Merges CLR(1) states with identical core (ignoring lookahead differences). Same parser size as SLR but can parse more grammars. Risk: merging may introduce reduce-reduce conflicts (rare for well-designed grammars). Trade-off: **practical efficiency vs power.**

topics.name: Compiler 2: Attribute Grammars & Intermediate Code
topics.description: CL14–CL22 (9 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 2 (Sat B3, 2.5 hr) + Free Day 2 (2 hr: SLR+LALR practice). Attribute grammars: synthesized (bottom-up) vs inherited (top-down). S-attributed (only synthesized). L-attributed (inherited flows left-to-right). Syntax-Directed Translation (SDT). Intermediate code: 3-address code (TAC), quadruples vs triples. Basic blocks, Control Flow Graph, local optimizations.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 2 (Sat B3): S-attributed + L-attributed problems
Free Day 2: SLR + LALR practice (SLR target: zero errors)

learningTip: **Synthesized vs Inherited:** Synthesized: value computed from children (bottom-up, works with LR parsing). Inherited: value passed from parent/siblings (top-down flow). ALL S-attributed grammars are also L-attributed.

**TAC (3-address code):** Three operands max: x = y op z, x = op y, x = y, goto L, if x relop y goto L. Supports quadruples (op, arg1, arg2, result) and triples (op, arg1, arg2) with implicit result numbering.
mustNotMiss: **S-attributed:** Only synthesized attributes. Can be evaluated during LR parsing (bottom-up). YACC actions use S-attributed by default.

**L-attributed:** Inherited + synthesized, but inherited attributes of a symbol can only depend on attributes of symbols to its LEFT in the production. Can be evaluated during LL(1) parsing (top-down).
**Basic block:** Maximal sequence of straight-line code (no branches into middle, single entry/exit). Basic blocks form nodes of Control Flow Graph.

interviewQuestions.question: Is the following grammar S-attributed? S → E; E → E+T {E.val=E1.val+T.val} | T {E.val=T.val}; T → num {T.val=num.val}.
interviewQuestions.answer: All attributes (val) are computed from children's values (addition of children's vals). **Yes, this is S-attributed (all synthesized).** It can be evaluated during LR parsing using a semantic stack.

topics.name: Compiler 3: Code Optimization & Data Flow Analysis
topics.description: CL23–CL28 (6 lectures × 2 hr) + DPP 3 (Sat B3, 2.5 hr). Local optimization: constant folding, dead code elimination, common subexpression elimination (CSE). Loop optimization: code motion (loop-invariant computation), strength reduction (multiply→add), induction variable elimination. ⚠️ CL21/CL28 slot: Live Variable Analysis — DO NOT SKIP.

practiceQuestions.name: DPP 3 (Sat B3): Data flow analysis problems
GATE PYQ: Live variable analysis

learningTip: **⚠️ LIVE VARIABLE ANALYSIS (CL21 — DO NOT SKIP):** Variable v is live at point p if there exists a path from p to a USE of v without an intervening definition. Used in register allocation. GATE 2021 had a 2-mark question on this that most aspirants missed.
**Reaching definitions (forward analysis):** A definition d reaches point p if there's a path from d to p with no redefinition of the same variable.
**Code motion (loop-invariant):** Identify computations inside loop whose result doesn't change. Move BEFORE the loop. Only safe if no exceptions can occur.
mustNotMiss: **Constant folding:** Evaluate constant expressions at compile time. x = 3*4 → x = 12.
**CSE (Common Subexpression Elimination):** If a+b computed twice with no change to a or b between computations, compute once and reuse.
**Strength reduction:** Replace expensive operation with cheaper equivalent. i*4 → i<<2 (multiply → shift). In loops: a[i*c] → use additive increment.

interviewQuestions.question: What is live variable analysis and why is it important for register allocation?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Live variable:** Variable v is live at point p if it WILL BE USED before being redefined on some path from p. **Register allocation:** Two variables with overlapping live ranges CANNOT share a register — they interfere. Build an interference graph (live variables that coexist → edge). Graph coloring with k colors → k registers. Variables that are never simultaneously live can share registers.

interviewQuestions.question: Identify which optimizations apply: `for(i=0;i<n;i++) { x = a+b; arr[i] = x*2; }`
interviewQuestions.answer: 1. **Code motion:** `x=a+b` is loop-invariant (a,b not modified) → move before loop. 2. **Strength reduction:** `x*2` → `x<<1` (or just use `x+x`). After code motion, the loop becomes: `x=(a+b)<<1; for(i=0;i<n;i++) arr[i]=x;` — cleaner and faster.

topics.name: Track A: Cross-Subject PYQ Revision & Final Assessment
topics.description: 5 PYQ revision sessions (2 hr each) + Phase 1+2 Assessment (Sat B1 slot, 3 hr: 50 Qs all subjects, 75 min, target ≥43/50). Sessions: DM 20 mixed PYQs (40 min), LA+Calc 10 each, P+Stats 15 PYQs, Compiler gate conditions, TOC 15 PYQs with 3 DFA constructions < 3 min each. Gate condition: ≥43/50 before entering Phase 3A.

practiceQuestions.name: DM Revision: 20 mixed PYQs all chapters (40 min)
Phase 1+2 Assessment: 50 Qs all subjects, 75 min (gate: ≥43/50)

learningTip: **Assessment gate condition:** Score ≥43/50 before Phase 3A. Score 39–42: repair Mon–Tue Wk 36. Below 39: 3-day repair block before PYQ Blitz.
**Revision strategy per session:** Use Error Log. Prioritize concepts where ✗ marks are concentrated. Don't study new material — only reinforce known weaknesses.

**Phase 1+2 covers ALL subjects.** Check Track A + Track B gates: DBMS PYQ ≥90%, C PYQ ≥88%, DS AVL <3min, Algo Master Theorem <20sec, OS Banker's <5min, CN Subnetting <2min, COA AMAT <3min, DL K-maps <2min, GA DPP ≥95%.
mustNotMiss: **No unresolved ✗ enters Phase 3.** Error Log must be current and fully resolved before the Phase 1+2 Assessment.
**Track A gate conditions summary:** DM ≥25/30 · Eng Math ≥33/40 · TOC PYQ ≥80% · Compiler: FIRST/FOLLOW <8min, SLR zero errors, CL21 not skipped.
**After assessment:** Phase 3A PYQ Blitz begins. The 3-week blitz is compressed from v7's 4 weeks — no slack.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the exam-day question-attempt strategy for GATE CS?
interviewQuestions.answer: **From guide §11:** (1) GA FIRST — all 10 questions in first 20 min. (2) Tier 1: DBMS→OS→DSA→CN. (3) Tier 2: Eng Math→COA→Algorithms. (4) Tier 3: Compiler→DM→TOC→DL. (5) 1-mark: attempt if you can eliminate 2 of 4. (6) 2-mark: only if ≥70% confident. (7) Last 15 min: flagged questions only. Never >5 min on single 2-mark question.

topics.name: Phase 3A: PYQ Blitz (3 weeks, subject-wise)
topics.description: 3 weeks × 31 hrs. All GATE 2015–2026 PYQs subject-by-subject. Nightly: 25 PYQs timed (2 hr). Weekday evening: Error Log + weak topic revision (1 hr). Sat B3: cross-subject 20-question mixed set. Sun B2: Error Log sweep (all ✗ resolved in writing). Pre-Mock 1 hard requirement: every ✗ in Error Log has written resolution.

practiceQuestions.name: Wk 36: DBMS+OS+C+DS+Algo PYQs (target ≥86% each)
Wk 37: CN+COA+DL+GA PYQs (tiered accuracy targets)

learningTip: **PYQ accuracy floor rule:** Any subject finishing below 80% — add a dedicated 2.5-hr session that SAME Sunday. Does not get deferred.
**A3 quick-reference sheet:** Build subject by subject as each blitz completes. Both sides of ONE A3 sheet only. Reserve ≥half for personal 'trap list' from your own Error Log.
**Dec 25 (Wk 38) is a study day.** Redistribute that day's PYQs to Mon–Thu. The exam is in February — no holidays in Phase 3.
mustNotMiss: **Hard requirement before Mock 1:** Every Error Log entry has a written resolution (topic, mistake, correct method). No exceptions.
**Wk 38 group (most marks):** Eng Math+DM+TOC+Compiler = ~42 marks combined. This is where the exam is won or lost in Phase 3A.
**PYQ sources order:** GATE Overflow (canonical) → Made Easy → ACE Academy. Do NOT attempt mocks series PYQs — save those for Phase 3B.

interviewQuestions.question: What is the purpose of the Error Log discipline throughout the preparation?
interviewQuestions.answer: The Error Log forces **deliberate practice on specific failure modes.** Every ✗ recorded with (1) topic/concept, (2) exact mistake made, (3) correct method written in full. Pattern recognition across the Error Log reveals systematic weaknesses (e.g., always wrong on Bellman-Ford negative cycle edge cases) vs random errors. Sun B3 is inviolably dedicated to Error Log deep review. No Error Log → repeat same mistakes on exam day.

topics.name: Phase 3B: Mock Simulation (10 Full 3-hr Mocks)
topics.description: 5 weeks × 31 hrs = 155 hrs. 10 full 3-hour mocks (2/week: Sat B1 + Sun B1). Each mock followed by complete post-mortem (score, subject-wise %, 3 most expensive errors, every ✗ reviewed with correct method). Weekday evenings: targeted problem-solving on weakest subject from previous mocks. Score ladder: ≥56→≥62→≥67→≥72→≥76. Mock 9+10 ≥76 = readiness signal for 80+ on exam day.

practiceQuestions.name: Mocks 1–4 (Wk 39–40): Calibration phase (target ≥56 → ≥62)
Mocks 9–10 (Wk 43): Final readiness check (target ≥76)

learningTip: **3-hour rule (from guide):** GATE is exactly 3 hours. A 2.5-hour mock loses all time-calibration. Both mocks each week MUST be full 3-hour sessions. Phone away. Virtual calculator only. Rough work on paper only.
**GA first — every mock from Mock 1.** Build the reflex. By Mock 10 it must be fully automatic. Virtual calculator only — GATE uses it.
**Mock sources (from guide):** GATE Overflow Full Mocks (highest quality) → Made Easy → ACE Academy. Final 2 mocks = actual GATE 2025 and 2026 papers used as untouched full mocks.
mustNotMiss: **Post-mortem protocol:** Score → subject-wise % → 3 most expensive errors identified → every ✗ written up with correct method. Sat B2+B3 and Sun B2 are for post-mortems.
**Wk 42 GA check:** If any mock scored <93% on GA → dedicate 1 full weekday to GA review. GA at 15 marks is non-negotiable.

interviewQuestions.question: What does 'GA FIRST every mock' achieve beyond just scoring on GA?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Psychological and strategic:** GA questions are the easiest marks — locking them in first eliminates anxiety of 'running out of time for easy marks'. Creates a positive start that improves performance on harder CS questions. The 15 marks from GA 'in bank' allows more aggressive flagging of hard 2-mark questions without panic. By Mock 10, the habit is automatic — replicates exactly on exam day.

topics.name: Phase 3C: Final Sprint & Exam Countdown
topics.description: Last 3 days of Wk 43 (Final Sprint, ~15 hrs) + Wk 44 Exam Countdown (~31 hrs). Sprint: A3 sheet + 15 Qs from weakest subjects. Sun Jan 31: half-mock (33 Qs, 45 min, full exam conditions) + post-mortem. STOP studying after half-mock. Wk 44: A3 sheet 20 min/day only. 9+ hours sleep nightly. Exam kit verified by Fri.

practiceQuestions.name: Phase 3C—Sprint: Half-mock 33 Qs, 45 min (final mock)
Exam Countdown: A3 sheet review (20 min/day, 7 days)

learningTip: **After half-mock on Sun Jan 31 morning:** STOP studying. A3 sheet once. Formula cards once. Rest. Walk. Eat well. 22:00 sleep. No new learning after Mock 10.

**A3 sheet (from guide §13):** Both sides of ONE A3 sheet. Contains: AMAT, pipeline CPI, TLB EAT, ALOHA, sliding window, OS deadlock conditions, NP chain, LL(1) conditions, flip-flop excitation tables, Bayes'. Plus: YOUR personal trap list from Error Log (reserve ≥half the sheet for this).
**Exam kit checklist (Fri before exam):** Admit card (printed + photocopied), government ID (photo), pen, pencil, eraser, water bottle. Centre + route + departure time verified.
mustNotMiss: **Arrival: 25 min early.** Settle, read instructions, virtual calculator test. No surprises.

**2-mark strategy (from guide):** Only attempt if ≥70% confident. Genuine 50-50: flag, skip, return in last 10 min. Never >5 min on any single 2-mark question. Change answer only if you identify a specific error in original working.
**Target: 80+ raw marks = AIR < 200 (based on GATE CS 2024–2026 cutoffs).** Tier 1 subjects (DBMS+OS+DSA+CN) consistently ≥85% across final mocks is the strongest predictor.

interviewQuestions.question: Why is no studying allowed after the final half-mock on Jan 31?
interviewQuestions.answer: **Three reasons:** (1) Sleep and rest are more valuable than marginal learning in the final 14 days — each hour of 9+ hrs sleep improves working memory and recall more than 1 hr of studying at this stage. (2) New material introduced in last 2 weeks increases anxiety risk (encountering it in exam without full confidence). (3) A3 sheet 20 min/day provides review without cognitive overload. The science: consolidation happens during sleep, not study.
